430 [South Capitol St.](/wiki/South_Capitol_Street "South Capitol Street") SE, Washington, D.C., U.S.

* [High School Democrats of America](/wiki/High_School_Democrats_of_America "High School Democrats of America") * [College Democrats of America](/wiki/College_Democrats_of_America "College Democrats of America")
[Young Democrats of America](/wiki/Young_Democrats_of_America "Young Democrats of America")
[National Federation of Democratic Women](/wiki/National_Federation_of_Democratic_Women "National Federation of Democratic Women")

* [Liberalism](/wiki/Modern_liberalism_in_the_United_States "Modern liberalism in the United States")[[7]](#cite_note-7) * **[Faction](/wiki/Factions_in_the_Democratic_Party_(United_States) "Factions in the Democratic Party (United States)")**: * [Progressivism](/wiki/Progressivism_in_the_United_States "Progressivism in the United States")[[10]](#cite_note-10)

* [Blue Dog Coalition](/wiki/Blue_Dog_Coalition "Blue Dog Coalition") * [New Democrat Coalition](/wiki/New_Democrat_Coalition "New Democrat Coalition") * [Congressional Progressive Caucus](/wiki/Congressional_Progressive_Caucus "Congressional Progressive Caucus")

is the major [liberal](/wiki/Modern_liberalism_in_the_United_States "Modern liberalism in the United States") political party in the United States, sitting on the [center](/wiki/Centrism "Centrism") to [center-left](/wiki/Center-left "Center-left") of the [political spectrum](/wiki/Political_spectrum "Political spectrum"). Founded in 1828, it is the world's oldest active political party. Its main rival is the [Republican Party](/wiki/Republican_Party_(United_States) "Republican Party (United States)"), and since the 1850s both have dominated [American politics](/wiki/American_politics "American politics").

The **Democratic Party** is the major [liberal](/wiki/Modern_liberalism_in_the_United_States "Modern liberalism in the United States") political party in the United States, sitting on the [center](/wiki/Centrism "Centrism") to [center-left](/wiki/Center-left "Center-left") of the [political spectrum](/wiki/Political_spectrum "Political spectrum"). Founded in 1828, it is the world's oldest active political party. Its main rival is the [Republican Party](/wiki/Republican_Party_(United_States) "Republican Party (United States)"), and since the 1850s both have dominated [American politics](/wiki/American_politics "American politics").

The Democratic Party initially supported [Jacksonian democracy](/wiki/Jacksonian_democracy "Jacksonian democracy"), [agrarianism](/wiki/Agrarianism "Agrarianism"), and [geographical expansionism](/wiki/Manifest_destiny "Manifest destiny"), while opposing [a national bank](/wiki/Bank_War "Bank War") and high [tariffs](/wiki/Tariff "Tariff"). Democrats won six of the eight presidential elections from 1828 to 1856, losing twice to the [Whigs](/wiki/Whig_Party_(United_States) "Whig Party (United States)"). In 1860, the party split into [Northern](/wiki/Northern_Democrats "Northern Democrats") and [Southern](/wiki/Southern_Democrats "Southern Democrats") factions over [slavery](/wiki/Slavery_in_the_United_States "Slavery in the United States"). The party remained dominated by agrarian interests, contrasting with Republican support for the [big business](/wiki/Big_business "Big business") of the [Gilded Age](/wiki/Gilded_Age "Gilded Age"). Democratic candidates won the presidency only twice[[b]](#cite_note-37) between 1860 and 1908, although they won the popular vote two more times in that period. During the [Progressive Era](/wiki/Progressive_Era "Progressive Era"), some [factions of the party](/wiki/Factions_in_the_Democratic_Party_(United_States) "Factions in the Democratic Party (United States)") supported [progressive](/wiki/Progressivism_in_the_United_States "Progressivism in the United States") reforms, with [Woodrow Wilson](/wiki/Woodrow_Wilson "Woodrow Wilson") being elected president in 1912 and 1916.

In 1932, [Franklin D. Roosevelt](/wiki/Franklin_D._Roosevelt "Franklin D. Roosevelt") was elected president after campaigning on a strong response to the [Great Depression](/wiki/Great_Depression "Great Depression"). His [New Deal](/wiki/New_Deal "New Deal") programs created [a broad Democratic coalition](/wiki/New_Deal_coalition "New Deal coalition") that united white Southerners, Northern workers, [labor unions](/wiki/Labor_unions_in_the_United_States "Labor unions in the United States"), [African Americans](/wiki/African_Americans "African Americans"), [Catholic](/wiki/Catholic_Church_in_the_United_States "Catholic Church in the United States") and [Jewish communities](/wiki/American_Jews "American Jews"), progressives, and [liberals](/wiki/Modern_liberalism_in_the_United_States "Modern liberalism in the United States"). From the late 1930s, [a conservative minority](/wiki/Conservative_coalition "Conservative coalition") in the party's Southern wing joined with Republicans to slow and stop further progressive domestic reforms.[[36]](#cite_note-38) After the [civil rights movement](/wiki/Civil_rights_movement "Civil rights movement") and [Great Society](/wiki/Great_Society "Great Society") era of progressive legislation under [Lyndon B. Johnson](/wiki/Lyndon_B._Johnson "Lyndon B. Johnson"), who was often able to overcome the conservative coalition in the 1960s, many white Southerners [switched to the Republican Party](/wiki/Southern_strategy "Southern strategy") as the Northeastern states became more reliably Democratic.[[37]](#cite_note-39)[[38]](#cite_note-gallup2010-40) The party's labor union element has weakened since the 1970s amid [deindustrialization](/wiki/Deindustrialization "Deindustrialization"), and during the 1980s it lost many white working-class voters to the Republicans under [Ronald Reagan](/wiki/Ronald_Reagan "Ronald Reagan"). The election of [Bill Clinton](/wiki/Bill_Clinton "Bill Clinton") in 1992 marked a shift for the party toward [centrism](/wiki/Centrism "Centrism") and the [Third Way](/wiki/Third_Way "Third Way"), shifting its economic stance toward [market-based policies](/wiki/Neoliberalism "Neoliberalism").[[39]](#cite_note-Hale-1995-41)[[40]](#cite_note-Wills-1997-42)[[41]](#cite_note-43) [Barack Obama](/wiki/Barack_Obama "Barack Obama") oversaw the party's passage of the [Affordable Care Act](/wiki/Affordable_Care_Act "Affordable Care Act") in 2010.[[42]](#cite_note-44)[[43]](#cite_note-45)

In the 21st century, the Democratic Party's strongest demographics are [urban voters](/wiki/Urban%E2%80%93rural_political_divide "Urban–rural political divide"), [college graduates](/wiki/Educational_attainment_in_the_United_States "Educational attainment in the United States") (especially those with [graduate degrees](/wiki/Postgraduate_education "Postgraduate education")), [African Americans](/wiki/African_Americans "African Americans"), women, [younger voters](/wiki/Youth_vote_in_the_United_States "Youth vote in the United States"), irreligious voters, the unmarried and [LGBTQ people](/wiki/LGBTQ_people_in_the_United_States "LGBTQ people in the United States").[[44]](#cite_note-46) On social issues, it advocates for [abortion rights](/wiki/Abortion-rights_movements "Abortion-rights movements"),[[45]](#cite_note-Traister-2023-47) [gun control](/wiki/Gun_control "Gun control"),[[46]](#cite_note-48) [LGBTQ rights](/wiki/LGBTQ_rights_in_the_United_States "LGBTQ rights in the United States"),[[47]](#cite_note-NPR-2012a-49) action on [climate change](/wiki/Climate_change "Climate change"),[[48]](#cite_note-50) and the [legalization of marijuana](/wiki/Legality_of_cannabis_by_U.S._jurisdiction "Legality of cannabis by U.S. jurisdiction"). On economic issues, the party favors [healthcare reform](/wiki/Healthcare_reform_in_the_United_States "Healthcare reform in the United States"), [paid sick leave](/wiki/Paid_sick_leave "Paid sick leave"), [paid family leave](/wiki/Paid_family_leave "Paid family leave") and [supporting unions](/wiki/Unionization "Unionization").[[49]](#cite_note-Miranda_Ollstein-2022-51)[[50]](#cite_note-Goodnough-2019-52)[[51]](#cite_note-53)[[52]](#cite_note-jlevy-54)[[53]](#cite_note-U.S._Department_of_State-55) In foreign policy, the party supports [liberal internationalism](/wiki/Liberal_internationalism "Liberal internationalism") and [aid to Ukraine](/wiki/Military_aid_to_Ukraine_during_the_Russo-Ukrainian_war "Military aid to Ukraine during the Russo-Ukrainian war"), as well as tougher stances against China and Russia.

## History

Main article: [History of the Democratic Party (United States)](/wiki/History_of_the_Democratic_Party_(United_States) "History of the Democratic Party (United States)")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/97/Development_of_Political_Parties_in_the_United_States.svg/250px-Development_of_Political_Parties_in_the_United_States.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Development_of_Political_Parties_in_the_United_States.svg)

Development of political parties in the United States

Democratic Party officials often trace its origins to the [Democratic-Republican Party](/wiki/Democratic-Republican_Party "Democratic-Republican Party"), founded by [Thomas Jefferson](/wiki/Thomas_Jefferson "Thomas Jefferson"), [James Madison](/wiki/James_Madison "James Madison") and other influential opponents of the conservative [Federalists](/wiki/Federalist_Party "Federalist Party") in 1792.[[54]](#cite_note-US_Congress-1991-56)[[55]](#cite_note-57) That party died out before the modern Democratic Party was organized;[[56]](#cite_note-58) the Jeffersonian party also inspired the [Whigs](/wiki/Whig_Party_(United_States) "Whig Party (United States)") and modern Republicans.[[57]](#cite_note-59) Historians argue that the modern Democratic Party was first organized in the late 1820s with the election of war hero [Andrew Jackson](/wiki/Andrew_Jackson "Andrew Jackson")[[58]](#cite_note-Kazin-2022-60) of Tennessee, making it the world's oldest active political party.[[59]](#cite_note-Lucas-2014-61)[[60]](#cite_note-Janda-2010-62)[[58]](#cite_note-Kazin-2022-60) It was predominately built by [Martin Van Buren](/wiki/Martin_Van_Buren "Martin Van Buren"), who assembled a wide cadre of politicians in every state behind Jackson.[[59]](#cite_note-Lucas-2014-61)[[58]](#cite_note-Kazin-2022-60)

Since the nomination of [William Jennings Bryan](/wiki/William_Jennings_Bryan "William Jennings Bryan") in 1896, the party has generally positioned itself to the left of the [Republican Party](/wiki/Republican_Party_(United_States) "Republican Party (United States)") on economic issues. Democrats have been more liberal on civil rights since 1948, although conservative factions within the Democratic Party that opposed them persisted in the South until the 1960s. On foreign policy, both parties have changed positions several times.[[61]](#cite_note-63)

### Background

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/43/Andrew_jackson_head.jpg/250px-Andrew_jackson_head.jpg)](/wiki/File:Andrew_jackson_head.jpg)

[Andrew Jackson](/wiki/Andrew_Jackson "Andrew Jackson") was the seventh president (1829–1837) and the first Democratic president.

The Democratic Party evolved from the [Jeffersonian Republican](/wiki/Jeffersonian_Republican "Jeffersonian Republican") or [Democratic-Republican Party](/wiki/Democratic-Republican_Party "Democratic-Republican Party") organized by Jefferson and Madison in opposition to the Federalist Party.[[62]](#cite_note-64) The Democratic-Republican Party favored [republicanism](/wiki/Republicanism_in_the_United_States "Republicanism in the United States"), a weak [federal government](/wiki/Federal_government_of_the_United_States "Federal government of the United States"), [states' rights](/wiki/States%27_rights "States' rights"), agrarian interests (especially Southern planters), and strict adherence to the Constitution. The party opposed a national bank and [Great Britain](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Great_Britain "Kingdom of Great Britain").[[63]](#cite_note-65) After the [War of 1812](/wiki/War_of_1812 "War of 1812"), the Federalists virtually disappeared and the only national political party left was the Democratic-Republicans, which was prone to splinter along regional lines.[[64]](#cite_note-66) The era of one-party rule in the United States, known as the [Era of Good Feelings](/wiki/Era_of_Good_Feelings "Era of Good Feelings"), lasted from 1816 until 1828, when Andrew Jackson became president. Jackson and Martin Van Buren worked with allies in each state to form a new Democratic Party on a national basis. In the 1830s, the [Whig Party](/wiki/Whig_Party_(United_States) "Whig Party (United States)") coalesced into the main rival to the Democrats.

When exactly the Democratic Party formed is still debated among historians, with many putting forth the 1828 date of the creation of a federal structure for the various Jacksonian movements as the foundation date, however, it could also be argued that the foundation of these Jacksonian groups could be the foundation date. In that case, the Democratic Party would be formed on December 23, 1823, when the Greensburg Committee read the *Greensburg Resolution* outside the [Westmoreland County Courthouse](/wiki/Westmoreland_County_Courthouse "Westmoreland County Courthouse") in [Greensburg, Pennsylvania](/wiki/Greensburg,_Pennsylvania "Greensburg, Pennsylvania"). The committee consisted of five of Greensburg's most prominent political figures, the brothers [Jacob M. Wise](/wiki/Jacob_M._Wise "Jacob M. Wise") (state senator), [John H. Wise](/wiki/John_H._Wise_(Pennsylvania_politician) "John H. Wise (Pennsylvania politician)") (state representative and brigadier general), and Frederick A. Wise (owner and editor of the *[Westmoreland Republican](/wiki/Westmoreland_Republican "Westmoreland Republican")*), alongside [David Marchand](/wiki/David_Marchand "David Marchand") (state representative), and [James Clarke](/wiki/James_Clarke_(Pennsylvania_politician) "James Clarke (Pennsylvania politician)") (state representative). The *Greensburg Resolution* was the first published call for Jackson to run for President with the committee being the first overtly "Jacksonian" organization, dubbed the 'origin' of the Jackson movement that turned into the Democratic Party.[[65]](#cite_note-67)

The event that transformed the Jacksonians from just another faction of the Democratic-Republican party into a divergent political force would be the so-called "[corrupt bargain](/wiki/Corrupt_bargain "Corrupt bargain")" of 1824, where, despite winning the most popular and electoral votes, the House of Representatives did not confirm Jackson as the newly elected president, instead [Henry Clay](/wiki/Henry_Clay "Henry Clay"), who was both a candidate and the speaker of the house, [whipped](/wiki/Whip_(politics) "Whip (politics)") his supporters in congress to vote for the runner-up, [John Quincy Adams](/wiki/John_Quincy_Adams "John Quincy Adams"), in exchange for Adams's naming Clay the Secretary of State. Jackson and his followers began to coalesce more seriously into a structured party for the next election in 1828.

Before 1860, the Democratic Party supported [expansive presidential power](/wiki/Unitary_executive_theory "Unitary executive theory"),[[66]](#cite_note-Holt-1992-68) [the interests](/wiki/Slave_Power "Slave Power") of [slave states](/wiki/Slave_states_and_free_states "Slave states and free states"),[[67]](#cite_note-Bates-2015-69) [agrarianism](/wiki/Agrarianism "Agrarianism"),[[68]](#cite_note-EB-2023-70) and [expansionism](/wiki/Expansionism "Expansionism"),[[68]](#cite_note-EB-2023-70) while opposing [a national bank](/wiki/Bank_War "Bank War") and high [tariffs](/wiki/Tariff "Tariff").[[68]](#cite_note-EB-2023-70)

### 19th century

#### Jacksonian Era

Further information: [Second Party System](/wiki/Second_Party_System "Second Party System") and [Third Party System](/wiki/Third_Party_System "Third Party System")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/ba/Martin_Van_Buren_crop.jpg/250px-Martin_Van_Buren_crop.jpg)](/wiki/File:Martin_Van_Buren_crop.jpg)

[Martin Van Buren](/wiki/Martin_Van_Buren "Martin Van Buren") was the eighth president (1837–1841).

The [Democratic-Republican Party](/wiki/Democratic-Republican_Party "Democratic-Republican Party") split over the choice of a successor to President [James Monroe](/wiki/James_Monroe "James Monroe").[[69]](#cite_note-71) The faction that supported many of the old [Jeffersonian principles](/wiki/Jeffersonian_democracy "Jeffersonian democracy"), led by Andrew Jackson and Martin Van Buren, became the modern Democratic Party.[[70]](#cite_note-72) Historian [Mary Beth Norton](/wiki/Mary_Beth_Norton "Mary Beth Norton") explains the transformation in 1828:

> Jacksonians believed the people's will had finally prevailed. Through a lavishly financed coalition of state parties, political leaders, and newspaper editors, a popular movement had elected the president. The Democrats became the nation's first well-organized national party ... and tight party organization became the hallmark of nineteenth-century American politics.[[71]](#cite_note-73)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/87/Polk_crop.jpg/250px-Polk_crop.jpg)](/wiki/File:Polk_crop.jpg)

[James K. Polk](/wiki/James_K._Polk "James K. Polk") was the 11th president (1845–1849). He significantly extended the territory of the United States.

Behind the platforms issued by state and national parties stood a widely shared political outlook that characterized the Democrats:

> The Democrats represented a wide range of views but shared a fundamental commitment to the Jeffersonian concept of an agrarian society. They viewed the central government as the enemy of individual liberty. The [1824 "corrupt bargain"](/wiki/Corrupt_bargain#Election_of_1824 "Corrupt bargain") had strengthened their suspicion of Washington politics. ... Jacksonians feared the concentration of economic and political power. They believed that government intervention in the economy benefited special-interest groups and created corporate monopolies that favored the rich. They sought to restore the independence of the individual—the artisan and the ordinary farmer—by ending federal support of banks and corporations and restricting the use of paper currency, which they distrusted. Their definition of the proper role of government tended to be negative, and Jackson's political power was largely expressed in negative acts. He exercised the veto more than all previous presidents combined. ... Nor did Jackson share reformers' humanitarian concerns. He had no sympathy for American Indians, initiating the removal of the Cherokees along the [Trail of Tears](/wiki/Trail_of_Tears "Trail of Tears").[[72]](#cite_note-74)

Opposing factions led by [Henry Clay](/wiki/Henry_Clay "Henry Clay") helped form the Whig Party. The Democratic Party had a small yet decisive advantage over the Whigs until the 1850s when the Whigs fell apart over the issue of slavery. In 1854, angry with the [Kansas–Nebraska Act](/wiki/Kansas%E2%80%93Nebraska_Act "Kansas–Nebraska Act"), anti-slavery Democrats left the party and joined Northern Whigs to form the [Republican Party](/wiki/History_of_the_United_States_Republican_Party "History of the United States Republican Party").[[73]](#cite_note-75)[[74]](#cite_note-76) Martin van Buren also helped found the [Free Soil Party](/wiki/Free_Soil_Party "Free Soil Party") to oppose the spread of slavery, running as its candidate in the [1848 presidential election](/wiki/1848_United_States_presidential_election "1848 United States presidential election"), before returning to the Democratic Party and staying loyal to the Union.[[75]](#cite_note-77)

#### U.S. Civil War

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e9/Stephen_A_Douglas_-_headshot.jpg/250px-Stephen_A_Douglas_-_headshot.jpg)](/wiki/File:Stephen_A_Douglas_-_headshot.jpg)

[Stephen A. Douglas](/wiki/Stephen_A._Douglas "Stephen A. Douglas") was a United States senator for Illinois.

The Democrats split over slavery, with Northern and Southern tickets in the [election of 1860](/wiki/1860_United_States_presidential_election "1860 United States presidential election"), in which the Republican Party gained ascendancy.[[76]](#cite_note-78) The radical pro-slavery [Fire-Eaters](/wiki/Fire-Eaters "Fire-Eaters") led walkouts at the two conventions when the delegates would not adopt a resolution supporting the extension of slavery into territories even if the voters of those territories did not want it. These [Southern Democrats](/wiki/1860_United_States_presidential_election#Constitutional_(Southern)_Democratic "1860 United States presidential election") nominated the pro-slavery incumbent vice president, [John C. Breckinridge](/wiki/John_C._Breckinridge "John C. Breckinridge") of Kentucky, for president and General [Joseph Lane](/wiki/Joseph_Lane "Joseph Lane"), of Oregon, for vice president. The [Northern Democrats](/wiki/1860_United_States_presidential_election#National_(Northern)_Democratic "1860 United States presidential election") nominated Senator [Stephen A. Douglas](/wiki/Stephen_A._Douglas "Stephen A. Douglas") of Illinois for president and former Georgia Governor [Herschel V. Johnson](/wiki/Herschel_Vespasian_Johnson "Herschel Vespasian Johnson") for vice president. This fracturing of the Democrats led to a Republican victory and [Abraham Lincoln](/wiki/Abraham_Lincoln "Abraham Lincoln") was elected the 16th president of the United States.[[77]](#cite_note-79)

As the [American Civil War](/wiki/American_Civil_War "American Civil War") broke out, Northern Democrats were divided into [War Democrats](/wiki/War_Democrats "War Democrats") and [Peace Democrats](/wiki/Copperheads_(politics) "Copperheads (politics)"). The [Confederate States of America](/wiki/Confederate_States_of_America "Confederate States of America") deliberately avoided organized political parties. Most War Democrats rallied to Republican president Abraham Lincoln and the Republicans' [National Union Party](/wiki/National_Union_Party_(United_States) "National Union Party (United States)") in the [election of 1864](/wiki/1864_United_States_presidential_election "1864 United States presidential election"), which featured [Andrew Johnson](/wiki/Andrew_Johnson "Andrew Johnson") on the Union ticket to attract fellow Democrats. Johnson replaced Lincoln in 1865, but he stayed independent of both parties.[[78]](#cite_note-80)

#### Reconstruction and Redemption

The Democrats benefited from white Southerners' resentment of [Reconstruction](/wiki/Reconstruction_era_of_the_United_States "Reconstruction era of the United States") after the war and consequent hostility to the Republican Party. After [Redeemers](/wiki/Redeemers "Redeemers") ended Reconstruction in the 1870s, and following the often extremely violent [disenfranchisement](/wiki/Disenfranchisement_after_the_Reconstruction_Era "Disenfranchisement after the Reconstruction Era") of African Americans led by such [white supremacist](/wiki/White_supremacy#Academic_use_of_the_term "White supremacy") Democratic politicians as [Benjamin Tillman](/wiki/Benjamin_Tillman "Benjamin Tillman") of [South Carolina](/wiki/South_Carolina "South Carolina") in the 1880s and 1890s, the South, voting Democratic, became known as the "[Solid South](/wiki/Solid_South "Solid South")". Although Republicans won all but two presidential elections, the Democrats remained competitive. The party was dominated by pro-business [Bourbon Democrats](/wiki/Bourbon_Democrat "Bourbon Democrat") led by [Samuel J. Tilden](/wiki/Samuel_J._Tilden "Samuel J. Tilden") and [Grover Cleveland](/wiki/Grover_Cleveland "Grover Cleveland"), who represented mercantile, banking, and railroad interests; opposed [imperialism](/wiki/Imperialism "Imperialism") and overseas expansion; fought for the [gold standard](/wiki/Gold_standard "Gold standard"); opposed [bimetallism](/wiki/Bimetallism "Bimetallism"); and crusaded against corruption, high taxes and tariffs. Cleveland was elected to non-consecutive presidential terms in [1884](/wiki/1884_United_States_presidential_election "1884 United States presidential election") and [1892](/wiki/1892_United_States_presidential_election "1892 United States presidential election").[[79]](#cite_note-81)

### 20th century

Further information: [Fourth Party System](/wiki/Fourth_Party_System "Fourth Party System"), [Fifth Party System](/wiki/Fifth_Party_System "Fifth Party System"), and [Sixth Party System](/wiki/Sixth_Party_System "Sixth Party System")

#### Progressive era

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/36/Breckinridgelong2.jpg/250px-Breckinridgelong2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Breckinridgelong2.jpg)

Leaders of the Democratic Party during the first half of the 20th century in 1913: William Jennings Bryan, [Josephus Daniels](/wiki/Josephus_Daniels "Josephus Daniels"), [Woodrow Wilson](/wiki/Woodrow_Wilson "Woodrow Wilson"), [Breckinridge Long](/wiki/Breckinridge_Long "Breckinridge Long"), [William Phillips](/wiki/William_Phillips_(diplomat) "William Phillips (diplomat)"), and Franklin D. Roosevelt

Agrarian Democrats demanding [free silver](/wiki/Free_silver "Free silver"), drawing on Populist ideas, overthrew the Bourbon Democrats in 1896 and nominated [William Jennings Bryan](/wiki/William_Jennings_Bryan "William Jennings Bryan") for the presidency (a nomination repeated by Democrats in 1900 and 1908). Bryan waged a vigorous campaign attacking Eastern moneyed interests, but he lost to Republican [William McKinley](/wiki/William_McKinley "William McKinley").[[80]](#cite_note-82)

The Democrats took control of the House in 1910, and [Woodrow Wilson](/wiki/Woodrow_Wilson "Woodrow Wilson") won election as president in 1912 (when the Republicans split) and 1916. Wilson effectively led Congress to put to rest the issues of tariffs, money, and antitrust, which had dominated politics for 40 years, with new progressive laws. He failed to secure Senate passage of the [Versailles Treaty](/wiki/Versailles_Treaty "Versailles Treaty") (ending the war with Germany and joining the League of Nations).[[81]](#cite_note-83) The weakened party was deeply divided by issues such as the KKK and prohibition in the 1920s. However, it did organize new ethnic voters in Northern cities.[[82]](#cite_note-84)

After [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I") ended and continuing through the [Great Depression](/wiki/Great_Depression "Great Depression"), the Democratic and Republican Parties both largely believed in [American exceptionalism](/wiki/American_exceptionalism "American exceptionalism") over European monarchies and [state socialism](/wiki/State_socialism "State socialism") that existed elsewhere in the world.[[83]](#cite_note-85)

#### 1930s–1960s and the rise of the New Deal coalition

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a5/RooseveltTruman1944poster.jpg/250px-RooseveltTruman1944poster.jpg)](/wiki/File:RooseveltTruman1944poster.jpg)

Franklin D. Roosevelt and Harry S. Truman, the 32nd and 33rd presidents (1933–1945; 1945–1953), featured on a campaign poster for the [1944 presidential election](/wiki/1944_United_States_presidential_election "1944 United States presidential election"); note the rooster logo of the Democratic Party (see [Names and Symbols](#Name_and_symbols) below)

The [Great Depression](/wiki/Great_Depression "Great Depression") in 1929 that began under Republican president [Herbert Hoover](/wiki/Herbert_Hoover "Herbert Hoover") and the Republican Congress set the stage for a more liberal government as the Democrats controlled the House of Representatives nearly uninterrupted from 1930 until 1994, the Senate for 44 of 48 years from 1930, and won most presidential elections until 1968. [Franklin D. Roosevelt](/wiki/Franklin_D._Roosevelt "Franklin D. Roosevelt"), elected to the presidency in 1932, came forth with federal government programs called the [New Deal](/wiki/New_Deal "New Deal"). New Deal liberalism meant the regulation of business (especially finance and banking) and the promotion of labor unions as well as federal spending to aid the unemployed, help distressed farmers, and undertake large-scale public works projects. It marked the start of the American welfare state.[[84]](#cite_note-86) The opponents, who stressed opposition to unions, support for business and low taxes, started calling themselves "conservatives".[[85]](#cite_note-87)

Until the 1980s, the Democratic Party was a coalition of two parties divided by the Mason–Dixon line: liberal Democrats in the North and culturally conservative voters in the South, who, though benefiting from many of the New Deal public-works projects, opposed increasing [civil rights](/wiki/Civil_and_political_rights "Civil and political rights") initiatives advocated by Northeastern liberals. The polarization grew stronger after Roosevelt died. Southern Democrats formed a key part of the bipartisan [conservative coalition](/wiki/Conservative_coalition "Conservative coalition") in an alliance with most of the Midwestern Republicans. The economically activist philosophy of Franklin D. Roosevelt, which has strongly influenced [American liberalism](/wiki/Liberalism_in_the_United_States "Liberalism in the United States"), shaped much of the party's economic agenda after 1932.[[86]](#cite_note-88) From the 1930s to the mid-1960s, the liberal [New Deal coalition](/wiki/New_Deal_coalition "New Deal coalition") usually controlled the presidency while the conservative coalition usually controlled Congress.[[87]](#cite_note-89)

#### 1960s–1980s and the collapse of the New Deal coalition

See also: [Civil Rights Movement](/wiki/Civil_Rights_Movement "Civil Rights Movement")

Issues facing parties and the United States after World War II included the [Cold War](/wiki/Cold_War "Cold War") and the [civil rights movement](/wiki/Civil_rights_movement "Civil rights movement"). Republicans attracted conservatives and, after the 1960s, white Southerners from the Democratic coalition with their use of the [Southern strategy](/wiki/Southern_strategy "Southern strategy") and resistance to New Deal and [Great Society](/wiki/Great_Society "Great Society") liberalism. Until the 1950s, African Americans had traditionally supported the Republican Party because of its anti-slavery civil rights policies. Following the passage of the [Civil Rights Act of 1964](/wiki/Civil_Rights_Act_of_1964 "Civil Rights Act of 1964") and [Voting Rights Act of 1965](/wiki/Voting_Rights_Act_of_1965 "Voting Rights Act of 1965"), the Southern states became more reliably Republican in presidential politics, while Northeastern states became more reliably Democratic.[[88]](#cite_note-Regional_Variations_in_the_Realignment_of_American_Politics,_1944–2004-90)[[89]](#cite_note-91)[[90]](#cite_note-Black-2003-92)[[91]](#cite_note-Activists_and_Partisan_Realignment-93) Studies show that Southern whites, who were a core constituency in the Democratic Party, shifted to the Republican Party due to [racial backlash](/wiki/White_backlash "White backlash") and [social conservatism](/wiki/Social_conservatism "Social conservatism").[[92]](#cite_note-Issue_Evolution-94)[[93]](#cite_note-Valentino-2005-95)[[94]](#cite_note-96)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c3/John_F._Kennedy%2C_White_House_color_photo_portrait.jpg/250px-John_F._Kennedy%2C_White_House_color_photo_portrait.jpg)](/wiki/File:John_F._Kennedy,_White_House_color_photo_portrait.jpg)

[John F. Kennedy](/wiki/John_F._Kennedy "John F. Kennedy"), the 35th president (1961–1963)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c3/37_Lyndon_Johnson_3x4.jpg/250px-37_Lyndon_Johnson_3x4.jpg)](/wiki/File:37_Lyndon_Johnson_3x4.jpg)

[Lyndon B. Johnson](/wiki/Lyndon_B._Johnson "Lyndon B. Johnson"), the 36th president (1963–1969)

The election of President [John F. Kennedy](/wiki/John_F._Kennedy "John F. Kennedy") from Massachusetts in 1960 partially reflected this shift. In the campaign, Kennedy attracted a new generation of younger voters. In his agenda dubbed the [New Frontier](/wiki/New_Frontier "New Frontier"), Kennedy introduced a host of social programs and public works projects, along with enhanced support of the [space program](/wiki/NASA "NASA"), proposing a crewed spacecraft [trip to the moon](/wiki/Apollo_11 "Apollo 11") by the end of the decade. He pushed for civil rights initiatives and proposed the [Civil Rights Act of 1964](/wiki/Civil_Rights_Act_of_1964 "Civil Rights Act of 1964"), but with his [assassination](/wiki/Assassination_of_John_F._Kennedy "Assassination of John F. Kennedy") in November 1963, he was not able to see its passage.[[95]](#cite_note-97)

Kennedy's successor [Lyndon B. Johnson](/wiki/Lyndon_B._Johnson "Lyndon B. Johnson") was able to persuade the largely conservative Congress to pass the Civil Rights Act of 1964, and with a more progressive Congress in 1965 passed much of the [Great Society](/wiki/Great_Society "Great Society"), including [Medicare](/wiki/Medicare_(United_States) "Medicare (United States)") and [Medicaid](/wiki/Medicaid "Medicaid"), which consisted of an array of social programs designed to help the poor, sick, and elderly. Kennedy and Johnson's advocacy of civil rights further solidified black support for the Democrats but had the effect of alienating Southern whites who would eventually gravitate toward the Republican Party, particularly after the election of [Ronald Reagan](/wiki/Ronald_Reagan "Ronald Reagan") to the presidency in 1980. Many conservative [Southern Democrats](/wiki/Southern_Democrats "Southern Democrats") defected to the [Republican Party](/wiki/Republican_Party_(United_States) "Republican Party (United States)"), beginning with the passage of the [Civil Rights Act of 1964](/wiki/Civil_Rights_Act_of_1964 "Civil Rights Act of 1964") and the general leftward shift of the party.[[96]](#cite_note-98)[[90]](#cite_note-Black-2003-92)[[91]](#cite_note-Activists_and_Partisan_Realignment-93)[[93]](#cite_note-Valentino-2005-95)

The United States' involvement in the [Vietnam War](/wiki/Vietnam_War "Vietnam War") in the 1960s was another divisive issue that further fractured the fault lines of the Democrats' coalition. After the [Gulf of Tonkin Resolution](/wiki/Gulf_of_Tonkin_Resolution "Gulf of Tonkin Resolution") in 1964, President Johnson committed a large contingency of combat troops to Vietnam, but the escalation failed to drive the [Viet Cong](/wiki/Viet_Cong "Viet Cong") from South Vietnam, resulting in an increasing [quagmire](/wiki/Quagmire_theory "Quagmire theory"), which by 1968 had become the subject of widespread anti-war protests in the United States and elsewhere. With increasing casualties and nightly news reports bringing home troubling images from Vietnam, the costly military engagement became increasingly unpopular, alienating many of the kinds of young voters that the Democrats had attracted in the early 1960s. The protests that year along with assassinations of [Martin Luther King Jr.](/wiki/Martin_Luther_King_Jr. "Martin Luther King Jr.") and Democratic presidential candidate Senator [Robert F. Kennedy](/wiki/Robert_F._Kennedy "Robert F. Kennedy") (younger brother of John F. Kennedy) climaxed in turbulence at the hotly-contested [Democratic National Convention](/wiki/1968_Democratic_National_Convention "1968 Democratic National Convention") that summer in Chicago (which amongst the ensuing turmoil inside and outside of the convention hall nominated Vice President [Hubert Humphrey](/wiki/Hubert_Humphrey "Hubert Humphrey")) in a series of events that proved to mark a significant turning point in the decline of the Democratic Party's broad coalition.[[97]](#cite_note-99)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5a/JimmyCarterPortrait2.jpg/250px-JimmyCarterPortrait2.jpg)](/wiki/File:JimmyCarterPortrait2.jpg)

[Jimmy Carter](/wiki/Jimmy_Carter "Jimmy Carter"), the 39th president (1977–1981)

Republican presidential nominee [Richard Nixon](/wiki/Richard_Nixon "Richard Nixon") was able to capitalize on the confusion of the Democrats that year and won the 1968 election to become the 37th president. He won re-election in a [landslide](/wiki/1972_United_States_presidential_election "1972 United States presidential election") in 1972 against Democratic nominee [George McGovern](/wiki/George_McGovern "George McGovern"), who like Robert F. Kennedy, reached out to the younger anti-war and counterculture voters, but unlike Kennedy, was not able to appeal to the party's more traditional white working-class constituencies. During Nixon's second term, his presidency was rocked by the [Watergate](/wiki/Watergate "Watergate") scandal, which forced him to resign in 1974. He was succeeded by vice president [Gerald Ford](/wiki/Gerald_Ford "Gerald Ford"), who served a brief tenure.

Watergate offered the Democrats an opportunity to recoup, and their nominee [Jimmy Carter](/wiki/Jimmy_Carter "Jimmy Carter") won the 1976 presidential election. With the initial support of [evangelical](/wiki/Evangelical "Evangelical") Christian voters in the South, Carter was temporarily able to reunite the disparate factions within the party, but inflation and the [Iran Hostage Crisis](/wiki/Iran_Hostage_Crisis "Iran Hostage Crisis") of 1979–1980 took their toll, resulting in a [landslide](/wiki/1980_United_States_presidential_election "1980 United States presidential election") victory for Republican presidential nominee [Ronald Reagan](/wiki/Ronald_Reagan "Ronald Reagan") in 1980, which shifted the political landscape in favor of the Republicans for years to come. The influx of conservative Democrats into the Republican Party is often cited as a reason for the Republican Party's shift further to the right during the late 20th century as well as the shift of its base from the Northeast and Midwest to the South.[[98]](#cite_note-100)[[99]](#cite_note-101)

#### 1990s and Third Way centrism

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d3/Bill_Clinton.jpg/250px-Bill_Clinton.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bill_Clinton.jpg)

[Bill Clinton](/wiki/Bill_Clinton "Bill Clinton"), the 42nd president (1993–2001)

With the ascendancy of the Republicans under Ronald Reagan, the Democrats searched for ways to respond yet were unable to succeed by running traditional candidates, such as former vice president and Democratic presidential nominee [Walter Mondale](/wiki/Walter_Mondale "Walter Mondale") and Massachusetts governor [Michael Dukakis](/wiki/Michael_Dukakis "Michael Dukakis"), who lost to Reagan and [George H. W. Bush](/wiki/George_H._W._Bush "George H. W. Bush") in the [1984](/wiki/1984_United_States_presidential_election "1984 United States presidential election") and [1988 presidential elections](/wiki/1988_United_States_presidential_election "1988 United States presidential election"), respectively. Many Democrats attached their hopes to the future star of [Gary Hart](/wiki/Gary_Hart "Gary Hart"), who had challenged Mondale in the [1984 primaries](/wiki/1984_Democratic_Party_presidential_primaries "1984 Democratic Party presidential primaries") running on a theme of "New Ideas"; and in the subsequent [1988 primaries](/wiki/1988_Democratic_Party_presidential_primaries "1988 Democratic Party presidential primaries") became the de facto front-runner and virtual "shoo-in" for the Democratic presidential nomination before a sex scandal ended his campaign. The party nevertheless began to seek out a younger generation of leaders, who like Hart had been inspired by the pragmatic idealism of John F. Kennedy.[[100]](#cite_note-102)

Arkansas governor [Bill Clinton](/wiki/Bill_Clinton "Bill Clinton") was one such figure, who was [elected](/wiki/1992_United_States_presidential_election "1992 United States presidential election") president in 1992 as the Democratic nominee. The [Democratic Leadership Council](/wiki/Democratic_Leadership_Council "Democratic Leadership Council") was a campaign organization connected to Clinton that advocated a [realignment](/wiki/Political_realignment "Political realignment") and [triangulation](/wiki/Triangulation_(politics) "Triangulation (politics)") under the re-branded "[New Democrat](/wiki/New_Democrats_(United_States) "New Democrats (United States)")" label.[[101]](#cite_note-Geismer-2019-103)[[39]](#cite_note-Hale-1995-41)[[40]](#cite_note-Wills-1997-42) The party adopted a synthesis of [neoliberal](/wiki/Neoliberalism "Neoliberalism") [economic policies](/wiki/Economic_policies "Economic policies") with [cultural liberalism](/wiki/Cultural_liberalism "Cultural liberalism"), with the voter base after Reagan having shifted considerably to the [right](/wiki/Right_(politics) "Right (politics)").[[101]](#cite_note-Geismer-2019-103) In an effort to appeal both to liberals and to fiscal conservatives, Democrats began to advocate for a [balanced budget](/wiki/Balanced_budget "Balanced budget") and [market economy](/wiki/Market_economy "Market economy") tempered by [government intervention](/wiki/Economic_interventionism "Economic interventionism") ([mixed economy](/wiki/Mixed_economy "Mixed economy")), along with a continued emphasis on [social justice](/wiki/Social_justice "Social justice") and [affirmative action](/wiki/Affirmative_action "Affirmative action"). The economic policy adopted by the Democratic Party, including the former [Clinton administration](/wiki/Presidency_of_Bill_Clinton "Presidency of Bill Clinton"), has been referred to as "[Third Way](/wiki/Third_Way "Third Way")".

The Democrats lost control of Congress in the [1994 elections](/wiki/Republican_Revolution "Republican Revolution") to the Republicans. However, in [1996](/wiki/1996_United_States_Presidential_Election "1996 United States Presidential Election"), Clinton was re-elected; he was the first Democratic president since Franklin D. Roosevelt to win a second full term. In December 1998, Republicans in the House of Representatives [impeached Clinton](/wiki/Impeachment_of_Bill_Clinton "Impeachment of Bill Clinton") for his role in the [Clinton–Lewinsky scandal](/wiki/Clinton%E2%80%93Lewinsky_scandal "Clinton–Lewinsky scandal"), but was [acquitted](/wiki/Impeachment_trial_of_Bill_Clinton "Impeachment trial of Bill Clinton") by the Senate in February 1999.[[102]](#cite_note-104) Clinton's vice president [Al Gore](/wiki/Al_Gore "Al Gore") ran to succeed him as president, and won the popular vote, but after a controversial election dispute over a Florida recount settled by the [U.S. Supreme Court](/wiki/U.S._Supreme_Court "U.S. Supreme Court") (which [ruled 5–4 in favor of Bush](/wiki/Bush_v._Gore "Bush v. Gore")), he lost the [2000 election](/wiki/2000_United_States_Presidential_Election "2000 United States Presidential Election") to Republican opponent [George W. Bush](/wiki/George_W._Bush "George W. Bush") in the [Electoral College](/wiki/United_States_Electoral_College "United States Electoral College").[[103]](#cite_note-Cornell-BushvGore-105)

### 21st century

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8d/President_Barack_Obama.jpg/250px-President_Barack_Obama.jpg)](/wiki/File:President_Barack_Obama.jpg)

[Barack Obama](/wiki/Barack_Obama "Barack Obama"), the 44th president (2009–2017)

In the wake of the [September 11 attacks](/wiki/September_11_attacks "September 11 attacks") as well as the growing concern over [global warming](/wiki/Global_warming "Global warming"), some of the party's key issues in the early 21st century have included combating [terrorism](/wiki/Terrorism "Terrorism") while preserving human rights, expanding access to health care, labor rights, and environmental protection. Democrats regained majority control of both the House and the Senate in the [2006 elections](/wiki/United_States_general_elections,_2006 "United States general elections, 2006"). [Barack Obama](/wiki/Barack_Obama "Barack Obama") won the Democratic Party's nomination and was elected as the first [African-American president](/wiki/African_heritage_of_presidents_of_the_United_States#Barack_Obama "African heritage of presidents of the United States") in 2008. Under the Obama presidency, the party moved forward with reforms including an [economic stimulus](/wiki/American_Recovery_and_Reinvestment_Act_of_2009 "American Recovery and Reinvestment Act of 2009") package, the [Dodd–Frank](/wiki/Dodd-Frank_Act "Dodd-Frank Act") financial reform act, and, in its biggest impact, [reshaped](/wiki/Healthcare_reform_in_the_United_States "Healthcare reform in the United States") the nation's [healthcare](/wiki/Healthcare_in_the_United_States "Healthcare in the United States") with the [Affordable Care Act](/wiki/Affordable_Care_Act "Affordable Care Act").[[104]](#cite_note-106)[[105]](#cite_note-Dayen_2024-107)

#### 2000s–2010s and the Obama era

In the [2010 midterm elections](/wiki/2010_United_States_elections "2010 United States elections"), the Democratic Party lost control of the House as well as its majorities in several state legislatures and governorships. The 2010 elections also marked the end of the Democratic Party's electoral dominance in the [Southern United States](/wiki/Southern_United_States "Southern United States").[[106]](#cite_note-The_long_goodbye-108)

In the [2012 elections](/wiki/2012_United_States_presidential_election "2012 United States presidential election"), President Obama was re-elected, but the party remained in the minority in the House of Representatives and lost control of the Senate in the [2014 midterm elections](/wiki/2014_United_States_elections "2014 United States elections"). After the [2016 election](/wiki/2016_United_States_presidential_election "2016 United States presidential election") of [Donald Trump](/wiki/Donald_Trump "Donald Trump"), who lost the popular vote to Democratic nominee [Hillary Clinton](/wiki/Hillary_Clinton "Hillary Clinton"), the Democratic Party transitioned into the role of an opposition party and held neither the presidency nor Congress for two years.[[107]](#cite_note-Revolt_of_the_Rust_Belt-109) However, the party won back the House in the [2018 midterm elections](/wiki/2018_United_States_House_of_Representatives_elections "2018 United States House of Representatives elections") under the leadership of [Nancy Pelosi](/wiki/Nancy_Pelosi "Nancy Pelosi").

Democrats were extremely critical of President Trump, particularly his policies on immigration, healthcare, as well as his response to the [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic "COVID-19 pandemic").[[108]](#cite_note-110)[[109]](#cite_note-111) In December 2019, Democrats in the House of Representatives [impeached Trump](/wiki/First_impeachment_of_Donald_Trump "First impeachment of Donald Trump"), although he was acquitted in the Republican-controlled Senate.[[110]](#cite_note-112)

#### 2020s and opposition to Trumpism

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/68/Joe_Biden_presidential_portrait.jpg/250px-Joe_Biden_presidential_portrait.jpg)](/wiki/File:Joe_Biden_presidential_portrait.jpg)

[Joe Biden](/wiki/Joe_Biden "Joe Biden"), the 46th president (2021–2025)

In November 2020, Democratic candidate [Joe Biden](/wiki/Joe_Biden "Joe Biden") defeated Trump to win the [2020 presidential election](/wiki/2020_United_States_presidential_election "2020 United States presidential election").[[111]](#cite_note-113) Biden's running mate, [Kamala Harris](/wiki/Kamala_Harris "Kamala Harris"), would be the [first female](/wiki/List_of_female_United_States_presidential_and_vice_presidential_candidates "List of female United States presidential and vice presidential candidates") and [person of African and South Asian descent](/wiki/List_of_African-American_United_States_presidential_and_vice_presidential_candidates "List of African-American United States presidential and vice presidential candidates") to become vice president in history. He began his term with extremely narrow Democratic majorities in the U.S. House and Senate.[[112]](#cite_note-114)[[113]](#cite_note-115) During the Biden presidency, the party was characterized as adopting an increasingly [progressive economic agenda](/wiki/Economic_progressivism "Economic progressivism").[[114]](#cite_note-Bridging_the_Blue_Divide-116) In 2022, Biden appointed [Ketanji Brown Jackson](/wiki/Ketanji_Brown_Jackson "Ketanji Brown Jackson"), the [first black woman](/wiki/List_of_African_American_federal_judges#United_States_Supreme_Court "List of African American federal judges") on the [Supreme Court](/wiki/Demographics_of_the_Supreme_Court_of_the_United_States "Demographics of the Supreme Court of the United States"). However, she was replacing liberal justice [Stephen Breyer](/wiki/Stephen_Breyer "Stephen Breyer"), thus she did not alter the court's 6–3 split between conservatives (the majority) and liberals.[[115]](#cite_note-117)[[116]](#cite_note-118)[[117]](#cite_note-119)[[118]](#cite_note-120) After *[Dobbs v. Jackson](/wiki/Dobbs_v._Jackson_Women%27s_Health_Organization "Dobbs v. Jackson Women's Health Organization")* (decided June 24, 2022), which led to [abortion bans in much of the country](/wiki/Abortion_law_in_the_United_States_by_state "Abortion law in the United States by state"), the Democratic Party rallied behind [abortion rights](/wiki/Abortion-rights_movements "Abortion-rights movements").[[45]](#cite_note-Traister-2023-47)

In the [2022 midterm elections](/wiki/2022_United_States_elections "2022 United States elections"), Democrats dramatically outperformed historical trends and a widely anticipated [red wave](/wiki/Wave_elections_in_the_United_States "Wave elections in the United States") did not materialize.[[119]](#cite_note-Tumulty_2022-121)[[120]](#cite_note-Blake_20222-122) The party only narrowly lost its majority in the U.S. House and expanded its majority in the U.S. Senate,[[121]](#cite_note-Kinery_2022-123)[[122]](#cite_note-Enter_2022-124)[[123]](#cite_note-Crampton_2022-125) along with several gains at the state level.[[124]](#cite_note-composition_2023_05_23_ncsl_org-126)[[125]](#cite_note-statehouse_2023_01_18_nytimes-127)[[126]](#cite_note-trifectas_2022_11_11_ap_foxnews-128)[[127]](#cite_note-states_2023_07_01_gazette-129)

In July 2024, after [a series of age and health concerns](/wiki/Age_and_health_concerns_about_Joe_Biden "Age and health concerns about Joe Biden"), Biden [withdrew from the presidential election](/wiki/Withdrawal_of_Joe_Biden_from_the_2024_United_States_presidential_election "Withdrawal of Joe Biden from the 2024 United States presidential election"), becoming the first incumbent president since [Lyndon B. Johnson in 1968](/wiki/Withdrawal_of_Lyndon_B._Johnson_from_the_1968_United_States_presidential_election "Withdrawal of Lyndon B. Johnson from the 1968 United States presidential election") to withdraw from running for reelection, the first since the 19th century to withdraw after serving only one term,[[c]](#cite_note-132) and the only one to ever withdraw after already winning [the primaries](/wiki/Democratic_Party_presidential_primaries "Democratic Party presidential primaries").[[128]](#cite_note-Klassen-2024-130)[[130]](#cite_note-133)

Vice President [Kamala Harris](/wiki/Kamala_Harris "Kamala Harris")—who became Biden's replacement on the ballot after his withdrawal from the race—became the [first black woman to be nominated by a major party](/wiki/List_of_African-American_United_States_presidential_and_vice_presidential_candidates#U.S._presidential_candidates:_party_nominees "List of African-American United States presidential and vice presidential candidates"), but she was defeated in the [2024 election](/wiki/2024_United_States_presidential_election "2024 United States presidential election") by Donald Trump. Harris lost the [electoral college](/wiki/United_States_electoral_college "United States electoral college") 312–226 (including all seven of the anticipated [swing states](/wiki/Swing_state "Swing state")) as well as the popular vote, becoming the first Democratic candidate to do so since [John Kerry](/wiki/John_Kerry "John Kerry") in 2004, amid what was a global anti-incumbent backlash.[[131]](#cite_note-graveyard-134)[[132]](#cite_note-135)[[133]](#cite_note-Global_Politics-136)[[134]](#cite_note-137)

#### Current status

As of 2026, Democrats hold 24 [state governorships](/wiki/List_of_United_States_governors "List of United States governors"), 17 [state legislatures](/wiki/List_of_United_States_state_legislatures "List of United States state legislatures"), 16 state government [trifectas](/wiki/Government_trifecta "Government trifecta"), and the mayorships in the majority of the country's major cities.[[135]](#cite_note-138) Three of the nine current [U.S. Supreme Court](/wiki/U.S._Supreme_Court "U.S. Supreme Court") justices were appointed by Democratic presidents. By registered members, the Democratic Party is the largest party in the U.S. and the [third largest in the world](/wiki/List_of_largest_political_parties "List of largest political parties"). All totaled, 16 Democrats have served as president of the United States.[[4]](#cite_note-sarnold-4)

## Name and symbols

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
|  | It has been suggested that mascots be [**split**](/wiki/Wikipedia:Splitting "Wikipedia:Splitting") out into another article. ([**Discuss**](/wiki/Talk:Political_parties_in_the_United_States#Article_on_party_mascots "Talk:Political parties in the United States")) *(August 2024)* |

"Democratic donkey" redirects here. For the statue, see [*Democratic Donkey* (statue)](/wiki/Democratic_Donkey_(statue) "Democratic Donkey (statue)").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/26/Democraticjackass.jpg/250px-Democraticjackass.jpg)](/wiki/File:Democraticjackass.jpg)

"A Live Jackass Kicking a Dead Lion" by Thomas Nast, *[Harper's Weekly](/wiki/Harper%27s_Magazine "Harper's Magazine")*, January 19, 1870

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/02/DemocraticLogo.svg/250px-DemocraticLogo.svg.png)](/wiki/File:DemocraticLogo.svg)

The donkey party logo remains a well-known symbol for the Democratic Party despite not being the official logo of the party.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/73/US_Democratic_Party_Logo.svg/250px-US_Democratic_Party_Logo.svg.png)](/wiki/File:US_Democratic_Party_Logo.svg)

In use since 2010, the letter "D" enclosed in a circle has been one of the Democratic Party's main symbols.

The [Democratic-Republican Party](/wiki/Democratic-Republican_Party "Democratic-Republican Party") splintered in 1824 into the short-lived [National Republican Party](/wiki/National_Republican_Party "National Republican Party") and the Jacksonian movement which in 1828 became the Democratic Party. During the Jacksonian era, the term "The Democracy" was in use by the party, but the name "Democratic Party" was eventually settled upon[[136]](#cite_note-139) and became the official name in 1844.[[137]](#cite_note-140) Members of the party are called "Democrats" or "Dems".

The most common mascot symbol for the party has been the donkey, or jackass.[[138]](#cite_note-141) [Andrew Jackson](/wiki/Andrew_Jackson "Andrew Jackson")'s enemies twisted his name to "jackass" as a term of ridicule regarding a stupid and stubborn animal. However, the Democrats liked the common-man implications and picked it up too, therefore the image persisted and evolved.[[139]](#cite_note-142) Its most lasting impression came from the cartoons of [Thomas Nast](/wiki/Thomas_Nast "Thomas Nast") from 1870 in *[Harper's Weekly](/wiki/Harper%27s_Magazine "Harper's Magazine")*. Cartoonists followed Nast and used the donkey to represent the Democrats and the elephant to represent the Republicans.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b1/Alabama_Democrats_logo_1904-1996.jpg/250px-Alabama_Democrats_logo_1904-1996.jpg)](/wiki/File:Alabama_Democrats_logo_1904-1996.jpg)

In many states, the logo of the Democratic Party was a rooster, for instance, in Alabama: Logo of the [Alabama Democratic Party](/wiki/Alabama_Democratic_Party "Alabama Democratic Party"), 1904–1966 (left) and 1966–1996 (right)[[140]](#cite_note-Ingram-143)[[141]](#cite_note-Bad_symbol_removed-144)

In the early 20th century, the traditional symbol of the Democratic Party in Indiana, Kentucky, Oklahoma, and Ohio was the rooster, as opposed to the Republican eagle.[[142]](#cite_note-Barbour_County-2008-145) The rooster was also adopted as an official symbol of the national Democratic Party.[[143]](#cite_note-146) In 1904, the Alabama Democratic Party chose, as the logo to put on its ballots, a rooster with the motto "White supremacy – For the right."[[144]](#cite_note-147) The words "White supremacy" were replaced with "Democrats" in 1966.[[145]](#cite_note-148)[[140]](#cite_note-Ingram-143) In 1996, the Alabama Democratic Party dropped the rooster, citing racist and white supremacist connotations linked with the symbol.[[141]](#cite_note-Bad_symbol_removed-144) The rooster symbol still appears on Oklahoma, Kentucky, Indiana, and West Virginia [ballots](/wiki/Ballot "Ballot").[[142]](#cite_note-Barbour_County-2008-145) In New York, the Democratic ballot symbol is a five-pointed star.[[146]](#cite_note-149)

Although both major political parties (and many minor ones) use the traditional American colors of red, white, and blue in their marketing and representations, since the [election night 2000](/wiki/2000_United_States_presidential_election "2000 United States presidential election") blue has become the identifying color for the Democratic Party while red has become the identifying color for the Republican Party. That night, for the first time all major broadcast television networks used the same color scheme for the electoral map: [blue states](/wiki/Red_states_and_blue_states "Red states and blue states") for [Al Gore](/wiki/Al_Gore "Al Gore") (Democratic nominee) and red states for [George W. Bush](/wiki/George_W._Bush "George W. Bush") (Republican nominee). Since then, the color blue has been widely used by the media to represent the party. This is contrary to common practice outside of the United States where blue is the traditional color of the right and red the color of the left.[[147]](#cite_note-WP_Nov_2004-150)

In 2010, the party introduced a new logo, with a blue capital "D" enclosed in a blue circle.[[148]](#cite_note-151) It remained the party's primary brand mark, with minor changes to the shade of blue, for 15 years. In 2025, a new logo was introduced, which incorporates a white donkey facing to the right instead of the left, with three blue stars in the center instead of four, on a blue background.[[149]](#cite_note-152)

[Jefferson-Jackson Day](/wiki/Jefferson-Jackson_Day "Jefferson-Jackson Day") is the annual fundraising event (dinner) held by Democratic Party organizations across the United States.[[150]](#cite_note-153) It is named after presidents [Thomas Jefferson](/wiki/Thomas_Jefferson "Thomas Jefferson") and [Andrew Jackson](/wiki/Andrew_Jackson "Andrew Jackson"), whom the party regards as its distinguished early leaders.

The song "[Happy Days Are Here Again](/wiki/Happy_Days_Are_Here_Again "Happy Days Are Here Again")" is the unofficial song of the Democratic Party. It was used prominently when [Franklin D. Roosevelt](/wiki/Franklin_D._Roosevelt "Franklin D. Roosevelt") was nominated for president at the [1932 Democratic National Convention](/wiki/1932_Democratic_National_Convention "1932 Democratic National Convention") and remains a sentimental favorite for Democrats. For example, [Paul Shaffer](/wiki/Paul_Shaffer "Paul Shaffer") played the theme on the *[Late Show with David Letterman](/wiki/Late_Show_with_David_Letterman "Late Show with David Letterman")* after the Democrats won Congress in 2006. "[Don't Stop](/wiki/Don%27t_Stop_(Fleetwood_Mac_song) "Don't Stop (Fleetwood Mac song)")" by [Fleetwood Mac](/wiki/Fleetwood_Mac "Fleetwood Mac") was adopted by [Bill Clinton](/wiki/Bill_Clinton "Bill Clinton")'s presidential campaign in 1992 and has endured as a popular Democratic song. The emotionally similar song "[Beautiful Day](/wiki/Beautiful_Day "Beautiful Day")" by the band [U2](/wiki/U2 "U2") has also become a favorite theme song for Democratic candidates. [John Kerry](/wiki/John_Kerry "John Kerry") used the song during his 2004 presidential campaign and several Democratic congressional candidates used it as a celebratory tune in 2006.[[151]](#cite_note-154)[[152]](#cite_note-155)

As a traditional anthem for its presidential nominating convention, [Aaron Copland](/wiki/Aaron_Copland "Aaron Copland")'s "[Fanfare for the Common Man](/wiki/Fanfare_for_the_Common_Man "Fanfare for the Common Man")" was performed at the beginning of the [1976 Democratic National Convention](/wiki/1976_Democratic_National_Convention "1976 Democratic National Convention").[[153]](#cite_note-156)[[154]](#cite_note-157)

## Structure

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fa/Democratic_National_Headquarters_%2853832041544%29.jpg/250px-Democratic_National_Headquarters_%2853832041544%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Democratic_National_Headquarters_(53832041544).jpg)

Democratic National Headquarters (2024)

### National committee

See also: [List of Democratic National Conventions](/wiki/List_of_Democratic_National_Conventions "List of Democratic National Conventions")

The [Democratic National Committee](/wiki/Democratic_National_Committee "Democratic National Committee") (DNC) is responsible for promoting Democratic campaign activities. While the DNC is responsible for overseeing the process of writing the Democratic Platform, the DNC is more focused on campaign and organizational strategy than [public policy](/wiki/Public_policy "Public policy"). In presidential elections, it supervises the [Democratic National Convention](/wiki/Democratic_National_Convention "Democratic National Convention"). The national convention is subject to the charter of the party and the ultimate authority within the Democratic Party when it is in session, with the DNC running the party's organization at other times. Since February 1, 2025, the DNC has been chaired by [Ken Martin](/wiki/Ken_Martin "Ken Martin").[[155]](#cite_note-158)

### State parties

Main article: [List of state parties of the Democratic Party (United States)](/wiki/List_of_state_parties_of_the_Democratic_Party_(United_States) "List of state parties of the Democratic Party (United States)")

Each state also has a state committee, made up of elected committee members as well as ex officio committee members (usually elected officials and representatives of major constituencies), which in turn elects a chair. County, town, city, and ward committees generally are composed of individuals elected at the local level. State and local committees often coordinate campaign activities within their jurisdiction, oversee local conventions, and in some cases primaries or caucuses, and may have a role in nominating candidates for elected office under state law. Rarely do they have much direct funding, but in 2005 DNC Chairman Dean began a program (called the "50 State Strategy") of using DNC national funds to assist all state parties and pay for full-time professional staffers.[[156]](#cite_note-159)

In addition, state-level party committees operate in the territories of [American Samoa](/wiki/American_Samoa_Democratic_Party "American Samoa Democratic Party"), [Guam](/wiki/Democratic_Party_of_Guam "Democratic Party of Guam"), and [Virgin Islands](/wiki/Democratic_Party_of_the_Virgin_Islands "Democratic Party of the Virgin Islands"), the commonwealths of [Northern Mariana Islands](/wiki/Democratic_Party_(Northern_Mariana_Islands) "Democratic Party (Northern Mariana Islands)") and [Puerto Rico](/wiki/Democratic_Party_(Puerto_Rico) "Democratic Party (Puerto Rico)"), and the [District of Columbia](/wiki/District_of_Columbia_Democratic_State_Committee "District of Columbia Democratic State Committee"), with all but Puerto Rico being active in nominating candidates for both presidential and territorial contests, while Puerto Rico's Democratic Party is organized only to nominate presidential candidates. The [Democrats Abroad](/wiki/Democrats_Abroad "Democrats Abroad") committee is organized by American voters who reside outside of U.S. territory to nominate presidential candidates. All such party committees are accorded recognition as state parties and are allowed to elect both members to the National Committee as well as delegates to the National Convention.

### Major party committees and groups

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/30/Barack_Obama_Speaks_to_College_Democrats.jpg/250px-Barack_Obama_Speaks_to_College_Democrats.jpg)](/wiki/File:Barack_Obama_Speaks_to_College_Democrats.jpg)

Then-Senator Obama speaking to College Democrats of America in 2007

The [Democratic Congressional Campaign Committee](/wiki/Democratic_Congressional_Campaign_Committee "Democratic Congressional Campaign Committee") (DCCC) assists party candidates in House races and is chaired by Representative [Suzan DelBene](/wiki/Suzan_DelBene "Suzan DelBene") of [Washington](/wiki/Washington_(state) "Washington (state)"). Similarly, the [Democratic Senatorial Campaign Committee](/wiki/Democratic_Senatorial_Campaign_Committee "Democratic Senatorial Campaign Committee") (DSCC), chaired by Senator [Gary Peters](/wiki/Gary_Peters "Gary Peters") of Michigan, raises funds for Senate races. The [Democratic Legislative Campaign Committee](/wiki/Democratic_Legislative_Campaign_Committee "Democratic Legislative Campaign Committee") (DLCC), chaired by [Majority Leader of the New York State Senate](/wiki/Majority_Leader_of_the_New_York_State_Senate "Majority Leader of the New York State Senate") [Andrea Stewart-Cousins](/wiki/Andrea_Stewart-Cousins "Andrea Stewart-Cousins"), is a smaller organization that focuses on state legislative races. The [Democratic Governors Association](/wiki/Democratic_Governors_Association "Democratic Governors Association") (DGA) is an organization supporting the candidacies of Democratic gubernatorial nominees and incumbents. Likewise, the mayors of the largest cities and urban centers convene as the [National Conference of Democratic Mayors](/wiki/National_Conference_of_Democratic_Mayors "National Conference of Democratic Mayors").[[157]](#cite_note-160)

The DNC sponsors the [College Democrats of America](/wiki/College_Democrats_of_America "College Democrats of America") (CDA), a student-outreach organization with the goal of training and engaging a new generation of Democratic activists. [Democrats Abroad](/wiki/Democrats_Abroad "Democrats Abroad") is the organization for Americans living outside the United States. They work to advance the party's goals and encourage Americans living abroad to support the Democrats. The [Young Democrats of America](/wiki/Young_Democrats_of_America "Young Democrats of America") (YDA) and the [High School Democrats of America](/wiki/High_School_Democrats_of_America "High School Democrats of America") (HSDA) are young adult and youth-led organizations respectively that attempt to draw in and mobilize young people for Democratic candidates but operate outside of the DNC.

## Political positions

Main article: [Political positions of the Democratic Party (United States)](/wiki/Political_positions_of_the_Democratic_Party_(United_States) "Political positions of the Democratic Party (United States)")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d0/US_real_median_household_income_1967_-_2011.PNG/330px-US_real_median_household_income_1967_-_2011.PNG)](/wiki/File:US_real_median_household_income_1967_-_2011.PNG)

This graph shows the real median [U.S. household income](/wiki/Household_income_in_the_United_States "Household income in the United States") by race: 1967 to 2011, in 2011 dollars.[[158]](#cite_note-Income_by_Race-161)

The Democratic Party is widely described in [American sources](/wiki/News_media_in_the_United_States "News media in the United States") as either a [centrist](/wiki/Centrism "Centrism"),[[159]](#cite_note-162) or a [center-left](/wiki/Centre-left_politics "Centre-left politics") political party.[[160]](#cite_note-Brookings2019-163) Analysts including [Harold Meyerson](/wiki/Harold_Meyerson "Harold Meyerson") and [William Galston](/wiki/William_Galston "William Galston") note that many of its mainstream policy positions and prominent [factions](/wiki/Factions_in_the_Democratic_Party_(United_States) "Factions in the Democratic Party (United States)") would be classified as [centrist](/wiki/Centrism "Centrism") by international standards, in particular those of Europe, and they are often seen as more comparable to [liberal-centrist](/wiki/Liberalism "Liberalism") parties (for example parties associated with [ALDE](/wiki/Alliance_of_Liberals_and_Democrats_for_Europe_Party "Alliance of Liberals and Democrats for Europe Party")/[Renew](/wiki/Renew_Europe "Renew Europe") or the UK [Liberal Democrats](/wiki/Liberal_Democrats_(UK) "Liberal Democrats (UK)")) than to traditional [social-democratic](/wiki/Social_democracy "Social democracy") parties; the party also contains distinct [left-wing](/wiki/Left-wing_politics "Left-wing politics") subgroups (such as the "[Squad](/wiki/Squad_(U.S._Congress) "Squad (U.S. Congress)")") alongside more centrist coalitions within its broad electoral coalition.[[161]](#cite_note-Prospect2024-164)[[162]](#cite_note-Brookings2021-165)[[163]](#cite_note-FiveThirtyEight2019-166) Political scientists Robert C. Sinclair and R. Jeffrey Melton described the Democratic Party as "slightly to the right of the largest Canadian party, the center-left [Liberal Party](/wiki/Canadian_Liberal_Party "Canadian Liberal Party")".[[164]](#cite_note-167)

The 21st century Democratic Party is unique and differs from other parties of similar profile in its ideological orientation, in part due to its [heterogenous](/wiki/Homogeneity_and_heterogeneity "Homogeneity and heterogeneity") demographic composition. In particular, the Democratic Party's ideology derives from being supported by both racial minorities, particularly [African Americans](/wiki/African_Americans "African Americans"), as well as white voters with high [educational attainment](/wiki/Educational_attainment_in_the_United_States "Educational attainment in the United States").[[165]](#cite_note-Harry_Enten-168)[[166]](#cite_note-Nate_Silver-169)

Its voting demographics are heavily educationally and racially-polarized, but not income polarized.[[167]](#cite_note-culture_trumps_economic_class-170) The Democratic Party is weakest among white voters without college degrees in the 21st century.[[168]](#cite_note-171) Higher educational attainment is strongly correlated with higher income and wealth, and also strongly correlated with increased ideological support for the Democratic Party's positions among white voters.[[169]](#cite_note-Abramowitz_2021-172) Ideologically, the Democratic Party is more diverse than the [Republican Party](/wiki/Republican_Party_(United_States) "Republican Party (United States)"), according to data collected by [Gallup](/wiki/Gallup,_Inc. "Gallup, Inc.").[[170]](#cite_note-173)

This derives in part from unique regional characteristics of the United States, particularly the [Southern United States](/wiki/Southern_United_States "Southern United States"). Racial polarization is extremely high in the Southern United States, with [black Southerners](/wiki/Black_Southerners "Black Southerners") almost entirely voting for the Democratic Party, and [white Southerners](/wiki/White_Southerners "White Southerners") almost entirely voting for the Republican Party.[[171]](#cite_note-Cohn-2014-174)[[172]](#cite_note-Dogs-175) Also, white Southerners with college degrees are strongly Republican, unlike in most of the rest of the country.[[173]](#cite_note-White_Vote_and_Educational_Polarization-176) [African Americans](/wiki/African_Americans "African Americans") continue to have the lowest incomes of any racial group in the United States.[[158]](#cite_note-Income_by_Race-161)

The Democratic Party's contemporary liberalism has its origins in the [Puritans](/wiki/Puritans "Puritans") of [New England](/wiki/New_England "New England"), with their emphasis on education and science dating back to the colonial era and the [Scientific Revolution](/wiki/Scientific_Revolution "Scientific Revolution"). This liberalism is older than the [classical liberalism](/wiki/Classical_liberalism "Classical liberalism") or [social democracy](/wiki/Social_democracy "Social democracy") of the 19th century.[[174]](#cite_note-177)

The Democratic Party's social positions derive from those of the [New Left](/wiki/New_Left "New Left"), that is [cultural liberalism](/wiki/Cultural_liberalism "Cultural liberalism"). These include [feminism](/wiki/Feminism "Feminism"), [LGBTQ rights](/wiki/LGBTQ_rights_in_the_United_States "LGBTQ rights in the United States"), [drug policy reforms](/wiki/Drug_liberalization "Drug liberalization"), and [environmentalism](/wiki/Environmentalism "Environmentalism").[[175]](#cite_note-178)[[176]](#cite_note-Carmines_and_Layman-179)[[177]](#cite_note-Kaufman-180)[[178]](#cite_note-181)[[179]](#cite_note-182) The party's platform favors a generous [welfare state](/wiki/Welfare_state "Welfare state") and a greater measure of social and economic equality.[[180]](#cite_note-Larry_E._Sullivan_2009_p_291-183) On social issues, it advocates for [the continued legality of abortion](/wiki/Abortion-rights_movements "Abortion-rights movements"),[[45]](#cite_note-Traister-2023-47) the [legalization of marijuana](/wiki/Legality_of_cannabis_by_U.S._jurisdiction "Legality of cannabis by U.S. jurisdiction"),[[181]](#cite_note-Gurley-2020-184) and LGBTQ rights.[[47]](#cite_note-NPR-2012a-49)

### Economic issues

The [social safety net](/wiki/Social_safety_net "Social safety net") and strong [labor unions](/wiki/Labor_unions_in_the_United_States "Labor unions in the United States") have been at the heart of Democratic economic policy since the [New Deal](/wiki/New_Deal "New Deal") in the 1930s.[[180]](#cite_note-Larry_E._Sullivan_2009_p_291-183) The Democratic Party's economic policy positions, as measured by votes in Congress, tend to align with those of the middle class.[[182]](#cite_note-Grossmann-2021-185)[[183]](#cite_note-186)[[184]](#cite_note-187)[[185]](#cite_note-188)[[186]](#cite_note-189) Democrats support a [progressive tax](/wiki/Progressive_tax "Progressive tax") system, higher [minimum wages](/wiki/Minimum_wage_in_the_United_States "Minimum wage in the United States"), [equal opportunity employment](/wiki/Equal_opportunity_employment "Equal opportunity employment"), [Social Security](/wiki/Social_Security_(United_States) "Social Security (United States)"), [universal health care](/wiki/Universal_health_care "Universal health care"), [public education](/wiki/Education_in_the_United_States "Education in the United States"), and [subsidized housing](/wiki/Subsidized_housing_in_the_United_States "Subsidized housing in the United States").[[180]](#cite_note-Larry_E._Sullivan_2009_p_291-183) They also support [infrastructure development](/wiki/Infrastructure_Investment_and_Jobs_Act "Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act") and clean energy investments to achieve economic development and job creation.[[187]](#cite_note-190)

Since the 1990s, the party has at times supported [centrist](/wiki/Centrism "Centrism") economic reforms that cut the size of government and reduced market regulations.[[188]](#cite_note-191) The party has generally rejected both [*laissez-faire* economics](/wiki/Laissez-faire "Laissez-faire") and [market socialism](/wiki/Market_socialism "Market socialism"), instead favoring [Keynesian economics](/wiki/Keynesian_economics "Keynesian economics") within a capitalist market-based system.[[189]](#cite_note-192) However, the party is not [social democratic](/wiki/Social_democratic "Social democratic") and does not base its policies on organized labor.[[190]](#cite_note-193) Since the 2020s, the party has also been distancing itself from predistributive economic policies such as job guarantees, minimum wage increases, protectionism and pro-union legislation.[[191]](#cite_note-194)

#### Fiscal policy

Democrats support a more [progressive tax](/wiki/Progressive_tax "Progressive tax") structure to provide more services and reduce [economic inequality](/wiki/Economic_inequality "Economic inequality") by making sure that the wealthiest Americans pay more in taxes.[[192]](#cite_note-how-high-195)
Democrats and Republicans traditionally take differing stances on eradicating poverty. Brady said "Our poverty level is the direct consequence of our weak social policies, which are a direct consequence of weak political actors".[[193]](#cite_note-196)
They oppose the cutting of social services, such as [Social Security](/wiki/Social_Security_(United_States) "Social Security (United States)"), [Medicare](/wiki/Medicare_(United_States) "Medicare (United States)"), and [Medicaid](/wiki/Medicaid "Medicaid"),[[194]](#cite_note-197) believing it to be harmful to efficiency and [social justice](/wiki/Social_justice "Social justice"). Democrats believe the benefits of social services in monetary and non-monetary terms are a more [productive labor](/wiki/Productive_and_unproductive_labour "Productive and unproductive labour") force and cultured population and believe that the benefits of this are greater than any benefits that could be derived from lower taxes, especially on top earners, or cuts to social services. Furthermore, Democrats see social services as essential toward providing [positive freedom](/wiki/Positive_liberty "Positive liberty"), freedom derived from economic opportunity. The Democratic-led House of Representatives reinstated the [PAYGO](/wiki/PAYGO "PAYGO") (pay-as-you-go) budget rule at the start of the [110th Congress](/wiki/110th_United_States_Congress "110th United States Congress").[[195]](#cite_note-198)

#### Minimum wage

See also: [Minimum wage in the United States](/wiki/Minimum_wage_in_the_United_States "Minimum wage in the United States")

The Democratic Party favors raising the [minimum wage](/wiki/Minimum_wage "Minimum wage"). The [Fair Minimum Wage Act of 2007](/wiki/Fair_Minimum_Wage_Act_of_2007 "Fair Minimum Wage Act of 2007") was an early component of the Democrats' agenda during the [110th Congress](/wiki/110th_United_States_Congress "110th United States Congress"). In 2006, the Democrats supported six state-ballot initiatives to increase the minimum wage and all six initiatives passed.[[196]](#cite_note-democrats.org-199)

In 2017, Senate Democrats introduced the Raise the Wage Act which would raise the minimum wage to $15 an hour by 2024.[[197]](#cite_note-200) In 2021, Democratic president [Joe Biden](/wiki/Joe_Biden "Joe Biden") proposed increasing the minimum wage to $15 by 2025.[[198]](#cite_note-201) In many states controlled by Democrats, the state minimum wage has been increased to a rate above the federal minimum wage.[[199]](#cite_note-202)

#### Health care

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2e/Obama_signing_health_care-20100323.jpg/250px-Obama_signing_health_care-20100323.jpg)](/wiki/File:Obama_signing_health_care-20100323.jpg)

President Obama signing the [Affordable Care Act](/wiki/Affordable_Care_Act "Affordable Care Act") into law in 2010

Democrats call for "affordable and quality health care" and favor moving toward [universal health care](/wiki/Universal_health_care "Universal health care") in a variety of forms to address rising healthcare costs.
