Progressive Democrats politicians favor a [single-payer program](/wiki/Single-payer_health_care "Single-payer health care") or [Medicare for All](/wiki/Medicare_for_All "Medicare for All"), while liberals prefer creating a [public health insurance option](/wiki/Public_health_insurance_option "Public health insurance option").[[50]](#cite_note-Goodnough-2019-52)

The [Affordable Care Act](/wiki/Affordable_Care_Act "Affordable Care Act"), signed into law by President [Barack Obama](/wiki/Barack_Obama "Barack Obama") on March 23, 2010, has been one of the most significant pushes for universal health care. As of December 2019, more than 20 million Americans have gained health insurance under the Affordable Care Act.[[200]](#cite_note-203)

#### Education

Democrats favor improving [public education](/wiki/Public_education "Public education") by raising school standards and reforming the [Head Start program](/wiki/Head_Start_(program) "Head Start (program)"). They also support [universal preschool](/wiki/Universal_preschool "Universal preschool"), expanding access to primary education, including through [charter schools](/wiki/Charter_schools "Charter schools"), and are generally opposed to [school voucher](/wiki/School_voucher "School voucher") programs. They call for addressing [student loan](/wiki/Student_loan "Student loan") debt and reforms to reduce college tuition.[[201]](#cite_note-204) Other proposals have included tuition-free public universities and reform of [standardized testing](/wiki/Standardized_testing "Standardized testing"). Democrats have the long-term aim of having publicly funded college education with low tuition fees (like in much of Europe and Canada), which would be available to every eligible American student. Alternatively, they encourage expanding access to post-secondary education by increasing state funding for student financial aid such as [Pell Grants](/wiki/Pell_Grants "Pell Grants") and [college tuition](/wiki/College_tuition "College tuition") [tax deductions](/wiki/Tax_deductions "Tax deductions").[[202]](#cite_note-205)

#### Environment

Main article: [Environmental policy of the United States](/wiki/Environmental_policy_of_the_United_States "Environmental policy of the United States")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a3/2009-_Pew_survey_-_is_climate_change_a_major_threat%2C_by_political_party.svg/250px-2009-_Pew_survey_-_is_climate_change_a_major_threat%2C_by_political_party.svg.png)](/wiki/File:2009-_Pew_survey_-_is_climate_change_a_major_threat,_by_political_party.svg)

Democrats and Republicans have diverged on the seriousness of the threat posed by climate change, with Democrats' assessment rising significantly in the mid-2010s.[[203]](#cite_note-PewClimateChange_20230418-206)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/65/2021_Survey_on_existence_of_global_warming_and_responsibility_for_climate_change_-_bar_chart.svg/250px-2021_Survey_on_existence_of_global_warming_and_responsibility_for_climate_change_-_bar_chart.svg.png)](/wiki/File:2021_Survey_on_existence_of_global_warming_and_responsibility_for_climate_change_-_bar_chart.svg)

The sharp divide over the existence of and responsibility for global warming and climate change falls largely along political lines. Overall, 60% of those surveyed said oil and gas companies were "completely or mostly responsible" for climate change.[[204]](#cite_note-Guardian_20211026-207)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3a/20220301_Opinions_by_political_party_-_Climate_change_causation_-_Action_for_carbon_neutral_2050_-_Pew_Research.svg/250px-20220301_Opinions_by_political_party_-_Climate_change_causation_-_Action_for_carbon_neutral_2050_-_Pew_Research.svg.png)](/wiki/File:20220301_Opinions_by_political_party_-_Climate_change_causation_-_Action_for_carbon_neutral_2050_-_Pew_Research.svg)

Opinion about human causation of climate change increased substantially with education among Democrats, but not among Republicans.[[205]](#cite_note-Pew_20220301-208) Conversely, opinions favoring becoming carbon neutral declined substantially with age among Republicans, but not among Democrats.[[205]](#cite_note-Pew_20220301-208)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1a/20220411_Support_for_policies_to_combat_climate_change%2C_by_political_party_-_Gallup_poll.svg/250px-20220411_Support_for_policies_to_combat_climate_change%2C_by_political_party_-_Gallup_poll.svg.png)](/wiki/File:20220411_Support_for_policies_to_combat_climate_change,_by_political_party_-_Gallup_poll.svg)

A broad range of policies to reduce greenhouse gas emissions has been proposed. Democrats' support for such policies consistently exceeds that of Republicans.[[206]](#cite_note-Gallup_20220411-209)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f3/202307_Survey_-_comfortable_with_solar_wind_nuclear_in_my_community.svg/250px-202307_Survey_-_comfortable_with_solar_wind_nuclear_in_my_community.svg.png)](/wiki/File:202307_Survey_-_comfortable_with_solar_wind_nuclear_in_my_community.svg)

Acceptance of wind and solar facilities in one's community is stronger among Democrats (blue), while acceptance of nuclear power plants is stronger among Republicans (red).[[207]](#cite_note-WashPost_20231003-210)

Democrats believe that the government should protect the environment and have a history of environmentalism. In more recent years, this stance has emphasized [renewable energy](/wiki/Renewable_energy "Renewable energy") generation as the basis for an improved economy, greater [national security](/wiki/National_security "National security"), and general environmental benefits.[[208]](#cite_note-211) The Democratic Party is substantially more likely than the Republican Party to support environmental regulation and policies that are supportive of renewable energy.[[209]](#cite_note-212)[[210]](#cite_note-213)

The Democratic Party also favors expansion of conservation lands and encourages open space and rail travel to relieve highway and airport congestion and improve air quality and the economy as it "believe[s] that communities, environmental interests, and the government should work together to protect resources while ensuring the vitality of local economies. Once Americans were led to believe they had to make a choice between the economy and the environment. They now know this is a false choice".[[211]](#cite_note-214)

The foremost environmental concern of the Democratic Party is [climate change](/wiki/Climate_change "Climate change"). Democrats, most notably former vice president [Al Gore](/wiki/Al_Gore "Al Gore"), have pressed for stern regulation of [greenhouse gases](/wiki/Greenhouse_gas "Greenhouse gas"). On [October 15, 2007](/wiki/2007_Nobel_Peace_Prize "2007 Nobel Peace Prize"), Gore won the [Nobel Peace Prize](/wiki/Nobel_Peace_Prize "Nobel Peace Prize") for his efforts to build greater knowledge about man-made climate change and lay the foundations for the measures needed to counteract it.[[212]](#cite_note-215)

#### Renewable energy and fossil fuels

Democrats have supported increased domestic [renewable energy](/wiki/Renewable_energy "Renewable energy") development, including wind and solar power farms, in an effort to reduce carbon pollution. The party's platform calls for an "all of the above" energy policy including clean energy, natural gas, and domestic oil, with the desire of becoming energy independent.[[196]](#cite_note-democrats.org-199) The party has supported higher taxes on [oil companies](/wiki/Oil_companies "Oil companies") and increased regulations on [coal power plants](/w/index.php?title=Coal_power_plants&action=edit&redlink=1 "Coal power plants (page does not exist)"), favoring a policy of reducing long-term reliance on [fossil fuels](/wiki/Fossil_fuels "Fossil fuels").[[213]](#cite_note-216)[[214]](#cite_note-217) Additionally, the party supports stricter [fuel emissions standards](/wiki/Fuel_emissions_standard "Fuel emissions standard") to prevent air pollution.

During his presidency, Joe Biden enacted the [Inflation Reduction Act of 2022](/wiki/Inflation_Reduction_Act_of_2022 "Inflation Reduction Act of 2022"), which is the largest allocation of funds for [addressing climate change](/wiki/Climate_change_mitigation "Climate change mitigation") in the history of the United States.[[215]](#cite_note-218)[[216]](#cite_note-219)[[217]](#cite_note-220)

#### Trade

Like the Republican Party, the Democratic Party has taken widely varying views on [international trade](/wiki/International_trade "International trade") throughout its history. The Democratic Party has usually been more supportive of [free trade](/wiki/Free_trade "Free trade") than the Republican Party.[[218]](#cite_note-221)[[219]](#cite_note-:1-222)[[220]](#cite_note-:2-223)[[221]](#cite_note-224)

The Democrats dominated the [Second Party System](/wiki/Second_Party_System "Second Party System") and set low tariffs designed to pay for the government but not protect industry. Their opponents, the Whigs, wanted high protective tariffs but usually were outvoted in Congress. Tariffs soon became a major political issue as the [Whigs](/wiki/Whig_Party_(United_States) "Whig Party (United States)") (1832–1852) and (after 1854) the Republicans wanted to protect their mostly Northern industries and constituents by voting for higher tariffs and the [Southern Democrats](/wiki/Southern_Democrats "Southern Democrats"), which had very little industry but imported many goods voted for lower tariffs. After the Second Party System ended in 1854, the Democrats lost control and the new Republican Party had its opportunity to raise rates.[[222]](#cite_note-225)

During the [Third Party System](/wiki/Third_Party_System "Third Party System"), Democratic president [Grover Cleveland](/wiki/Grover_Cleveland "Grover Cleveland") made low tariffs the centerpiece of Democratic Party policies, arguing that high tariffs were an unnecessary and unfair tax on consumers. The South and [West](/wiki/Western_United_States "Western United States") generally supported low tariffs, while the industrial [North](/wiki/Northern_United_States "Northern United States") supported high tariffs.[[223]](#cite_note-226) During the [Fourth Party System](/wiki/Fourth_Party_System "Fourth Party System"), Democratic president [Woodrow Wilson](/wiki/Woodrow_Wilson "Woodrow Wilson") made a drastic lowering of tariff rates a major priority for his presidency. The 1913 [Underwood Tariff](/wiki/Underwood_Tariff "Underwood Tariff") cut rates, and the new revenues generated by the [federal income tax](/wiki/Federal_income_tax "Federal income tax") made tariffs much less important in terms of economic impact and political rhetoric.[[224]](#cite_note-227)

During the [Fifth Party System](/wiki/Fifth_Party_System "Fifth Party System"), the [Reciprocal Tariff Act](/wiki/Reciprocal_Tariff_Act "Reciprocal Tariff Act") of 1934 was enacted during [FDR's](/wiki/Franklin_D._Roosevelt "Franklin D. Roosevelt") administration, marking a sharp departure from the era of [protectionism in the United States](/wiki/Protectionism_in_the_United_States "Protectionism in the United States"). American duties on foreign products declined from an average of 46% in 1934 to 12% by 1962.[[225]](#cite_note-Bailey-228) After World War II, the U.S. promoted the [General Agreement on Tariffs and Trade](/wiki/General_Agreement_on_Tariffs_and_Trade "General Agreement on Tariffs and Trade") (GATT) established in 1947 during the [Truman administration](/wiki/Presidency_of_Harry_S._Truman "Presidency of Harry S. Truman"), to minimize tariffs liberalize trade among all capitalist countries.[[226]](#cite_note-barton-229)[[227]](#cite_note-230)

In the 1990s, the Clinton administration and several prominent Democrats pushed through several agreements such as the [North American Free Trade Agreement](/wiki/North_American_Free_Trade_Agreement "North American Free Trade Agreement") (NAFTA).[[228]](#cite_note-231) [Barack Obama](/wiki/Barack_Obama "Barack Obama") signed several free trade agreements during his presidency while [Joe Biden](/wiki/Joe_Biden "Joe Biden") did not sign any free trade agreements during his presidency and increased some tariffs on China.[[229]](#cite_note-232)[[230]](#cite_note-233)

During Republican Donald Trump's two terms as president, the Democratic Party has been more in favor of free trade than the Republican Party.[[219]](#cite_note-:1-222)[[220]](#cite_note-:2-223) The Democratic Party remains supportive of the [USMCA](/wiki/United_States%E2%80%93Mexico%E2%80%93Canada_Agreement "United States–Mexico–Canada Agreement") free trade agreement with Mexico and Canada.[[231]](#cite_note-234)[[232]](#cite_note-235)

### Social issues

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/10/Shirley_Chisholm.jpg/250px-Shirley_Chisholm.jpg)](/wiki/File:Shirley_Chisholm.jpg)

[Shirley Chisholm](/wiki/Shirley_Chisholm "Shirley Chisholm") was the [first major-party African-American candidate](/wiki/African-American_candidates_for_President_of_the_United_States#Shirley_Chisholm "African-American candidates for President of the United States") to run nationwide primary campaigns.

The modern Democratic Party emphasizes [social equality](/wiki/Social_equality "Social equality") and [equal opportunity](/wiki/Equal_opportunity "Equal opportunity"). Democrats support [voting rights](/wiki/Voting_rights_in_the_United_States "Voting rights in the United States") and [minority rights](/wiki/Minority_rights "Minority rights"), including [LGBT rights](/wiki/LGBT_rights_in_the_United_States "LGBT rights in the United States").[[233]](#cite_note-236)[[234]](#cite_note-237)[[235]](#cite_note-238)[[236]](#cite_note-239) Democratic president [Lyndon B. Johnson](/wiki/Lyndon_B._Johnson "Lyndon B. Johnson") signed the [Civil Rights Act of 1964](/wiki/Civil_Rights_Act_of_1964 "Civil Rights Act of 1964"), which outlawed racial segregation. Carmines and Stimson wrote "the Democratic Party appropriated racial liberalism and assumed federal responsibility for ending racial discrimination."[[237]](#cite_note-240)[[238]](#cite_note-241)[[239]](#cite_note-242)

Ideological social elements in the party include [cultural liberalism](/wiki/Cultural_liberalism "Cultural liberalism"), [civil libertarianism](/wiki/Civil_libertarianism "Civil libertarianism"), and [feminism](/wiki/Feminism "Feminism").[[240]](#cite_note-243)[[241]](#cite_note-244)[[242]](#cite_note-245) Some Democratic social policies are immigration reform, [electoral reform](/wiki/Electoral_reform "Electoral reform"), and women's [reproductive rights](/wiki/Reproductive_rights "Reproductive rights").[[243]](#cite_note-246)[[244]](#cite_note-247)[[245]](#cite_note-248)

#### Equal opportunity

The Democratic Party is a staunch supporter of [equal opportunity](/wiki/Equal_opportunity "Equal opportunity") for all Americans regardless of sex, age, race, ethnicity, [sexual orientation](/wiki/Sexual_orientation "Sexual orientation"), [gender identity](/wiki/Gender_identity "Gender identity"), religion, creed, or national origin. The Democratic Party has broad appeal across most socioeconomic and ethnic demographics, as seen in recent exit polls.[[246]](#cite_note-249) Democrats also strongly support the [Americans with Disabilities Act](/wiki/Americans_with_Disabilities_Act_of_1990 "Americans with Disabilities Act of 1990") to prohibit discrimination against people based on physical or mental disability. As such, the Democrats pushed as well the [ADA Amendments Act of 2008](/wiki/ADA_Amendments_Act_of_2008 "ADA Amendments Act of 2008"), a disability rights expansion that became law.[[247]](#cite_note-250)

Most Democrats support [affirmative action](/wiki/Affirmative_action "Affirmative action") to further equal opportunity. However, in 2020 [57% voters in California](/wiki/2020_California_Proposition_16 "2020 California Proposition 16") voted to keep their state constitution's ban on affirmative action, despite Biden [winning 63% of the vote in California](/wiki/2020_United_States_presidential_election_in_California "2020 United States presidential election in California") in the same election.[[248]](#cite_note-251)

#### Voting rights

The party is very supportive of improving "voting rights" as well as election accuracy and accessibility.[[249]](#cite_note-252) They support extensions of voting time, including making election day a holiday. They support reforming the electoral system to eliminate [gerrymandering](/wiki/Gerrymandering "Gerrymandering"), abolishing the [electoral college](/wiki/United_States_Electoral_College "United States Electoral College"), as well as passing comprehensive [campaign finance reform](/wiki/Campaign_finance_reform_in_the_United_States "Campaign finance reform in the United States").[[250]](#cite_note-ontheissues.org-253)

#### Abortion and reproductive rights

See also: [Abortion in the United States](/wiki/Abortion_in_the_United_States "Abortion in the United States")

The Democratic position on abortion has changed significantly over time.[[251]](#cite_note-Williams-2015-254)[[252]](#cite_note-Williams-2022b-255) During the late 1960s and early 1970s, Republicans generally favored legalized abortion more than Democrats,[[253]](#cite_note-Halpern-2018-256) although significant heterogeneity could be found within both parties.[[254]](#cite_note-Taylor-2018-257) During this time, opposition to abortion tended to be concentrated within the political left in the United States. Liberal Protestants and Catholics (many of whom were Democratic voters) opposed abortion, while most conservative Protestants supported legal access to abortion services.[[251]](#cite_note-Williams-2015-254)[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*]

In its national platforms from 1992 to 2004, the Democratic Party has called for abortion to be "safe, legal and rare"—namely, keeping it legal by rejecting laws that allow governmental interference in abortion decisions and reducing the number of abortions by promoting both knowledge of reproduction and contraception and incentives for adoption. When Congress voted on the [Partial-Birth Abortion Ban Act](/wiki/Partial-Birth_Abortion_Ban_Act "Partial-Birth Abortion Ban Act") in 2003, congressional Democrats were split, with a minority (including former [Senate majority leader](/wiki/Party_leaders_of_the_United_States_Senate "Party leaders of the United States Senate") [Harry Reid](/wiki/Harry_Reid "Harry Reid")) supporting the ban and the majority of Democrats opposing the legislation.[[255]](#cite_note-258)

According to the 2020 Democratic Party platform, "Democrats believe every woman should be able to access high-quality reproductive health care services, including safe and legal abortion."[[256]](#cite_note-259)

After *[Roe v. Wade](/wiki/Roe_v._Wade "Roe v. Wade")* (1973) was overturned in *[Dobbs v. Jackson Women's Health Organization](/wiki/Dobbs_v._Jackson_Women%27s_Health_Organization "Dobbs v. Jackson Women's Health Organization")* (2022), Democratic-controlled states and ballot initiatives were able to ensure access to abortion. The number of abortions in the United States increased after *Dobbs*, due to the [right to travel](/wiki/Freedom_of_movement_under_United_States_law "Freedom of movement under United States law") between states.[[257]](#cite_note-260)[[258]](#cite_note-261)

#### Immigration

See also: [Immigration to the United States](/wiki/Immigration_to_the_United_States "Immigration to the United States") and [Illegal immigration to the United States](/wiki/Illegal_immigration_to_the_United_States "Illegal immigration to the United States")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ed/2000-_Border_apprehensions_at_southwest_border.svg/250px-2000-_Border_apprehensions_at_southwest_border.svg.png)](/wiki/File:2000-_Border_apprehensions_at_southwest_border.svg)

Apprehensions at the southwest border since 2000

Like the Republican Party, the Democratic Party has taken widely varying views on immigration throughout its history. Since the 1990s, the Democratic Party has been more supportive overall of immigration than the Republican Party.[[259]](#cite_note-262) Many Democratic politicians have called for systematic reform of the immigration system such that residents that have [come into the United States illegally](/wiki/Illegal_immigration_to_the_United_States "Illegal immigration to the United States") have a pathway to legal citizenship. President Obama remarked in November 2013 that he felt it was "long past time to fix our broken immigration system," particularly to allow "incredibly bright young people" who came over as students to become full citizens.[[260]](#cite_note-long-past-263) In 2013, Democrats in the Senate passed [S. 744](/wiki/Border_Security,_Economic_Opportunity,_and_Immigration_Modernization_Act_of_2013 "Border Security, Economic Opportunity, and Immigration Modernization Act of 2013"), which would reform immigration policy to allow citizenship for illegal immigrants in the United States. The law failed to pass in the House and was never re-introduced after the [113th Congress](/wiki/113th_Congress "113th Congress").[[261]](#cite_note-264)

Opposition to immigration has increased in the 2020s, with a majority of Democrats supporting increasing border security.[[262]](#cite_note-265)[[263]](#cite_note-266) In the [2024 presidential election](/wiki/2024_United_States_presidential_election "2024 United States presidential election"), Trump increased his vote share in counties along the [Mexico–United States border](/wiki/Mexico%E2%80%93United_States_border "Mexico–United States border"), including in [majority-Hispanic counties](/wiki/List_of_majority-Hispanic_or_Latino_counties_in_the_United_States "List of majority-Hispanic or Latino counties in the United States").[[264]](#cite_note-267)[[265]](#cite_note-268)

#### LGBTQ rights

See also: [LGBTQ rights in the United States](/wiki/LGBTQ_rights_in_the_United_States "LGBTQ rights in the United States")

The Democratic position on [LGBTQ rights](/wiki/LGBTQ_rights_in_the_United_States "LGBTQ rights in the United States") has changed significantly over time.[[266]](#cite_note-Igielnik-2022-269)[[267]](#cite_note-Lindberg-2022-270) Before the 2000s, like the Republicans, the Democratic Party often took positions hostile to LGBTQ rights. As of the 2020s, both voters and elected representatives within the Democratic Party are overwhelmingly supportive of [LGBT](/wiki/LGBT "LGBT") rights.[[266]](#cite_note-Igielnik-2022-269)

Support for same-sex marriage has steadily increased among the general public, including voters in both major parties, since the start of the 21st century. An April 2009 ABC News/*Washington Post* public opinion poll put support among Democrats at 62%.[[268]](#cite_note-271) A 2006 [Pew Research Center](/wiki/Pew_Research_Center "Pew Research Center") poll of Democrats found that 55% supported gays adopting children with 40% opposed while 70% support [gays in the military](/wiki/Sexual_orientation_and_military_service "Sexual orientation and military service"), with only 23% opposed.[[269]](#cite_note-272) Gallup polling from May 2009 stated that 82% of Democrats support open enlistment.[[270]](#cite_note-273) A 2023 Gallup public opinion poll found 84% of Democrats support same-sex marriage, compared to 71% support by the general public and 49% support by Republicans.[[271]](#cite_note-274)

The 2004 Democratic National Platform stated that marriage should be defined at the state level and it repudiated the [Federal Marriage Amendment](/wiki/Federal_Marriage_Amendment "Federal Marriage Amendment").[[272]](#cite_note-platform-275) [John Kerry](/wiki/John_Kerry "John Kerry"), the Democratic presidential nominee in 2004, did not support same-sex marriage in [his campaign.](/wiki/John_Kerry_2004_presidential_campaign "John Kerry 2004 presidential campaign") While not stating support of same-sex marriage, the 2008 platform called for repeal of the [Defense of Marriage Act](/wiki/Defense_of_Marriage_Act "Defense of Marriage Act"), which banned federal recognition of same-sex marriage and removed the need for interstate recognition, supported antidiscrimination laws and the extension of hate crime laws to LGBTQ people and opposed "don't ask, don't tell".[[273]](#cite_note-UCLA_press-276)[[274]](#cite_note-277) The 2012 platform included support for same-sex marriage and for the repeal of DOMA.[[47]](#cite_note-NPR-2012a-49)

On May 9, 2012, [Barack Obama](/wiki/Barack_Obama "Barack Obama") became the first sitting president to say he supports same-sex marriage.[[275]](#cite_note-278)[[276]](#cite_note-The_Huffington_Post-279) Previously, he had opposed restrictions on same-sex marriage such as the [Defense of Marriage Act](/wiki/Defense_of_Marriage_Act "Defense of Marriage Act"), which he promised to repeal,[[277]](#cite_note-LGBT-280) California's [Prop 8](/wiki/Prop_8 "Prop 8"),[[278]](#cite_note-281) and a [constitutional amendment](/wiki/Constitutional_amendment "Constitutional amendment") to ban same-sex marriage (which he opposed, saying that "decisions about marriage should be left to the states as they always have been"),[[279]](#cite_note-282) but also stated that he personally believed marriage to be between a man and a woman and that he favored civil unions that would "give same-sex couples equal legal rights and privileges as married couples".[[277]](#cite_note-LGBT-280) Earlier, when running for the Illinois Senate in 1996 he said, "I favor legalizing same-sex marriages, and would fight efforts to prohibit such marriages".[[280]](#cite_note-283) Former presidents [Bill Clinton](/wiki/Bill_Clinton "Bill Clinton")[[281]](#cite_note-284) and [Jimmy Carter](/wiki/Jimmy_Carter "Jimmy Carter")[[282]](#cite_note-285) along with former Democratic presidential nominees [Al Gore](/wiki/Al_Gore "Al Gore")[[283]](#cite_note-286) and [Michael Dukakis](/wiki/Michael_Dukakis "Michael Dukakis")[[284]](#cite_note-287) support same-sex marriage. President [Joe Biden](/wiki/Joe_Biden "Joe Biden") has supported [same-sex marriage](/wiki/Same-sex_marriage "Same-sex marriage") since 2012, when he became the highest-ranking government official to support it. In 2022, Biden signed the [Respect for Marriage Act](/wiki/Respect_for_Marriage_Act "Respect for Marriage Act"); the law repealed the Defense of Marriage Act, which Biden had voted for during his Senate tenure.[[285]](#cite_note-288)

#### Status of Puerto Rico and D.C.

The 2016 Democratic Party platform declares, regarding the status of Puerto Rico: "We are committed to addressing the extraordinary challenges faced by our fellow citizens in Puerto Rico. Many stem from the fundamental question of Puerto Rico's political status. Democrats believe that the people of Puerto Rico should determine their ultimate political status from permanent options that do not conflict with the Constitution, laws, and policies of the United States. Democrats are committed to promoting economic opportunity and good-paying jobs for the hardworking people of Puerto Rico. We also believe that Puerto Ricans must be treated equally by Medicare, Medicaid, and other programs that benefit families. Puerto Ricans should be able to vote for the people who make their laws, just as they should be treated equally. All American citizens, no matter where they reside, should have the right to vote for the president of the United States. Finally, we believe that federal officials must respect Puerto Rico's local self-government as laws are implemented and Puerto Rico's budget and debt are restructured so that it can get on a path towards stability and prosperity".[[286]](#cite_note-2016platform-289)

Also, it declares that regarding the status of the [District of Columbia](/wiki/District_of_Columbia "District of Columbia"): "Restoring our democracy also means finally passing statehood for the District of Columbia, so that the American citizens who reside in the nation's capital have full and equal congressional rights as well as the right to have the laws and budget of their local government respected without Congressional interference."[[286]](#cite_note-2016platform-289)

### Legal issues

#### Gun control

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a8/20210420_Gun_control_survey_by_political_party_-_Pew_Research.svg/500px-20210420_Gun_control_survey_by_political_party_-_Pew_Research.svg.png)](/wiki/File:20210420_Gun_control_survey_by_political_party_-_Pew_Research.svg)

U.S. opinion on gun control issues is deeply divided along political lines, as shown in this 2021 survey.[[287]](#cite_note-Pew_20210420-290)

With a stated goal of reducing crime and homicide, the Democratic Party has introduced various [gun control](/wiki/Gun_politics_in_the_United_States "Gun politics in the United States") measures, most notably the [Gun Control Act of 1968](/wiki/Gun_Control_Act_of_1968 "Gun Control Act of 1968"), the [Brady Bill](/wiki/Brady_Handgun_Violence_Prevention_Act "Brady Handgun Violence Prevention Act") of 1993 and the [Violent Crime Control and Law Enforcement Act](/wiki/Violent_Crime_Control_and_Law_Enforcement_Act "Violent Crime Control and Law Enforcement Act") (1994). In its national platform for [2008](/wiki/2008_Democratic_National_Convention "2008 Democratic National Convention"), the only statement explicitly favoring gun control was a plan calling for renewal of the 1994 [Assault Weapons Ban](/wiki/Federal_Assault_Weapons_Ban "Federal Assault Weapons Ban").[[288]](#cite_note-291) In 2022, Democratic president [Joe Biden](/wiki/Joe_Biden "Joe Biden") signed the [Bipartisan Safer Communities Act](/wiki/Bipartisan_Safer_Communities_Act "Bipartisan Safer Communities Act"), which, among other things, expanded background checks and provided incentives for states to pass [red flag laws](/wiki/Red_flag_laws "Red flag laws").[[289]](#cite_note-292)

The Democratic Party does not oppose gun ownership.[[290]](#cite_note-293) According to a 2023 Pew Research Center poll, 20% of Democrats owned firearms, compared to 32% of the general public and 45% of Republicans.[[291]](#cite_note-294)

#### Death penalty

See also: [Capital punishment in the United States](/wiki/Capital_punishment_in_the_United_States "Capital punishment in the United States")

The Democratic position on capital punishment has shifted multiple times over the decades. In 1968, Attorney General [Ramsey Clark](/wiki/Ramsey_Clark "Ramsey Clark"), representing the [Johnson Administration](/wiki/Presidency_of_Lyndon_B._Johnson "Presidency of Lyndon B. Johnson"), asked Congress to abolish the [federal death penalty](/wiki/Capital_punishment_by_the_United_States_federal_government "Capital punishment by the United States federal government").[[292]](#cite_note-295) In [1972](/wiki/1972_Democratic_National_Convention "1972 Democratic National Convention"), the Democratic Party platform called for the abolition of capital punishment.[[293]](#cite_note-1972-Platform-296) In 1988, Democratic Presidential nominee [Michael Dukakis](/wiki/Michael_Dukakis "Michael Dukakis")'s statement in the [1988 United States presidential debates](/wiki/1988_United_States_presidential_debates "1988 United States presidential debates") that he would oppose the death penalty even if his wife were raped and murdered was seen by many viewers as callous and emotionless, and was widely viewed as having contributed to his loss to [George H. W. Bush](/wiki/George_H._W._Bush "George H. W. Bush") in the general election.[[294]](#cite_note-297)

During his [presidential campaign](/wiki/Bill_Clinton_1992_presidential_campaign "Bill Clinton 1992 presidential campaign"), [Bill Clinton](/wiki/Bill_Clinton "Bill Clinton") sought to distance himself from his party's left flank through his strong support for the death penalty, including by personally supervising the execution of [Ricky Ray Rector](/wiki/Ricky_Ray_Rector "Ricky Ray Rector"), a lobotomized African-American man convicted of killing a police officer.[[295]](#cite_note-298)[[296]](#cite_note-299) During [Clinton's presidency](/wiki/Presidency_of_Bill_Clinton "Presidency of Bill Clinton"), Democrats led the expansion of the federal death penalty. These efforts were manifested in the 1994 [Violent Crime Control and Law Enforcement Act](/wiki/Violent_Crime_Control_and_Law_Enforcement_Act "Violent Crime Control and Law Enforcement Act"), which expanded the federal death penalty to around 60 offenses, and the [Antiterrorism and Effective Death Penalty Act of 1996](/wiki/Antiterrorism_and_Effective_Death_Penalty_Act_of_1996 "Antiterrorism and Effective Death Penalty Act of 1996"), which heavily limited appeals in death penalty cases.[[297]](#cite_note-300) The Democratic Party platforms of [1996](/wiki/1996_Democratic_National_Convention "1996 Democratic National Convention") and [2000](/wiki/2000_Democratic_National_Convention "2000 Democratic National Convention") supported capital punishment outright, while the Democratic Party platforms of 2008 and [2012](/wiki/2012_Democratic_National_Convention "2012 Democratic National Convention") warned against arbitrary application and the execution of innocents.[[298]](#cite_note-301)

In June [2016](/wiki/2016_Democratic_National_Convention "2016 Democratic National Convention"), the Democratic Platform Drafting Committee unanimously adopted an amendment to abolish the death penalty.[[299]](#cite_note-302) The 2020 Democratic Party platform reiterated the Party's opposition to capital punishment.[[300]](#cite_note-303)
The [2024 platform](/wiki/2024_Democratic_National_Convention "2024 Democratic National Convention") is the first since the [2004 platform](/wiki/2004_Democratic_National_Convention "2004 Democratic National Convention") that does not mention the death penalty, and the first since 2016 not to call for abolition.[[301]](#cite_note-304) However, on December 23, 2024, President Biden commuted the sentences of 37 out of 40 [federal death row inmates](/wiki/List_of_death_row_inmates_in_the_United_States#Federal "List of death row inmates in the United States") to life in prison without parole.[[302]](#cite_note-305)

#### Torture

Many Democrats are opposed to the [use of torture](/wiki/Torture_and_the_United_States "Torture and the United States") against individuals apprehended and held prisoner by the [United States military](/wiki/United_States_armed_forces "United States armed forces"), and hold that categorizing such prisoners as [unlawful combatants](/wiki/Unlawful_combatant "Unlawful combatant") does not release the United States from its obligations under the [Geneva Conventions](/wiki/Geneva_Conventions "Geneva Conventions"). Democrats contend that torture is inhumane, damages the United States' moral standing in the world, and produces questionable results. Democrats are largely against [waterboarding](/wiki/Waterboarding "Waterboarding").[[303]](#cite_note-306)

Torture became a divisive issue in the party after Barack Obama was elected president.[[304]](#cite_note-307)

#### Privacy

The Democratic Party believes that individuals should have a [right to privacy](/wiki/Privacy_law "Privacy law"). For example, many Democrats have opposed the [NSA warrantless surveillance of American citizens](/wiki/NSA_warrantless_surveillance_(2001%E2%80%932007) "NSA warrantless surveillance (2001–2007)").[[305]](#cite_note-308)[[306]](#cite_note-309)

Some Democratic officeholders have championed [consumer-protection](/wiki/Consumer_protection "Consumer protection") laws that limit the sharing of consumer data among corporations. Democrats have opposed [sodomy laws](/wiki/Sodomy_laws_in_the_United_States "Sodomy laws in the United States") since the 1972 platform, which stated that "Americans should be free to make their own choice of life-styles and private habits without being subject to discrimination or prosecution",[[293]](#cite_note-1972-Platform-296) and believe that government should not regulate [consensual](/wiki/Consensual "Consensual") noncommercial sexual conduct among adults as a matter of personal privacy.[[307]](#cite_note-310)

### Foreign policy issues

In foreign policy, the party supports [liberal internationalism](/wiki/Liberal_internationalism "Liberal internationalism") as well as tough stances against [China](/wiki/China "China") and [Russia](/wiki/Russia "Russia").[[308]](#cite_note-Ikenberry_2020-311)[[309]](#cite_note-Wong_2022-312)[[310]](#cite_note-The_World_Trump_Wants-313)

The foreign policy of the voters of the two major parties has largely overlapped since the 1990s. A Gallup poll in early 2013 showed broad agreement on the top issues, albeit with some divergence regarding human rights and international cooperation through agencies such as the United Nations.[[311]](#cite_note-314)

In June 2014, the Quinnipiac Poll asked Americans which foreign policy they preferred:

> A) The United States is doing too much in other countries around the world, and it is time to do less around the world and focus more on our own problems here at home.
> B) The United States must continue to push forward to promote democracy and freedom in other countries worldwide because these efforts make our own country more secure.

Democrats chose A over B by 65% to 32%; Republicans chose A over B by 56% to 39%; and independents chose A over B by 67% to 29%.[[312]](#cite_note-315)

#### Iran sanctions

See also: [United States sanctions against Iran](/wiki/United_States_sanctions_against_Iran "United States sanctions against Iran")

The Democratic Party has been critical of [Iran's nuclear program](/wiki/Nuclear_program_of_Iran "Nuclear program of Iran") and supported economic sanctions against the Iranian government. In 2013, the Democratic-led administration worked to reach a diplomatic agreement with the government of Iran to halt the Iranian nuclear program in exchange for [international economic sanction](/wiki/Sanctions_against_Iran "Sanctions against Iran") relief.[[313]](#cite_note-316) As of 2014[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Democratic_Party_(United_States)&action=edit), negotiations had been successful and the party called for more cooperation with Iran in the future.[[314]](#cite_note-317) In 2015, the Obama administration agreed to the [Joint Comprehensive Plan of Action](/wiki/Joint_Comprehensive_Plan_of_Action "Joint Comprehensive Plan of Action"), which provides sanction relief in exchange for international oversight of the [Iranian nuclear program](/wiki/Iranian_nuclear_program "Iranian nuclear program"). In February 2019, the Democratic National Committee passed a resolution calling on the United States to re-enter the JCPOA, from which President Trump withdrew the United States in 2018.[[315]](#cite_note-318)

#### Invasion of Afghanistan

See also: [Afghanistan–United States relations](/wiki/Afghanistan%E2%80%93United_States_relations "Afghanistan–United States relations") and [International public opinion on the war in Afghanistan](/wiki/International_public_opinion_on_the_war_in_Afghanistan "International public opinion on the war in Afghanistan")

Democrats in the House of Representatives and in the Senate near-unanimously voted for the [Authorization for Use of Military Force Against Terrorists](/wiki/Authorization_for_Use_of_Military_Force_Against_Terrorists "Authorization for Use of Military Force Against Terrorists") against "those responsible for the [recent attacks launched against the United States](/wiki/September_11_attacks "September 11 attacks")" in [Afghanistan](/wiki/Afghanistan "Afghanistan") in 2001, supporting the [NATO](/wiki/NATO "NATO") coalition [invasion of the nation](/wiki/Operation_Enduring_Freedom "Operation Enduring Freedom"). Most elected Democrats continued to support the [Afghanistan conflict](/wiki/War_in_Afghanistan_(2001%E2%80%93present) "War in Afghanistan (2001–present)") during George W. Bush's presidency.[[316]](#cite_note-319)[[317]](#cite_note-daily-320) During the [2008 Presidential Election](/wiki/2008_United_States_presidential_election "2008 United States presidential election"), then-candidate [Barack Obama](/wiki/Barack_Obama "Barack Obama") called for a "surge" of troops into Afghanistan.[[317]](#cite_note-daily-320) After winning the presidency, Obama followed through, sending additional troops to Afghanistan. Troop levels were 94,000 in December 2011 and kept falling, with a target of 68,000 by fall 2012.[[318]](#cite_note-321)

Support for the war among the American people diminished over time. Many Democrats changed their opinion over the course of the war, coming to oppose the continuation of the conflict.[[319]](#cite_note-holland-322)[[320]](#cite_note-edge-323) In July 2008, [Gallup](/wiki/Gallup_poll "Gallup poll") found that 41% of Democrats called the invasion a "mistake" while a 55% majority disagreed.[[320]](#cite_note-edge-323) A [CNN](/wiki/CNN "CNN") survey in August 2009 stated that a majority of Democrats opposed the war. CNN polling director Keating Holland said: "Nearly two thirds of Republicans support the war in Afghanistan. Three quarters of Democrats oppose the war".[[319]](#cite_note-holland-322)

During the [2020 Presidential Election](/wiki/2020_United_States_presidential_election "2020 United States presidential election"), then-candidate [Joe Biden](/wiki/Joe_Biden "Joe Biden") promised to "end the forever wars in Afghanistan and the Middle East."[[321]](#cite_note-324) Biden went on to win the election, and, in April 2021, he announced that he would withdraw all U.S. troops from Afghanistan by September 11 of that year.[[322]](#cite_note-325) The last troops left in August, bringing America's 20-year-long military campaign in the country to a close.[[323]](#cite_note-326) According to a 2023 AP-NORC poll, a majority of Democrats believed that the War in Afghanistan was not worth it.[[324]](#cite_note-327)

#### Israel

See also: [Israel–United States relations](/wiki/Israel%E2%80%93United_States_relations "Israel–United States relations")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b2/Shimon_Peres_observes_Binyamin_Nitanyahu_greeting_Barack_Obama.jpg/250px-Shimon_Peres_observes_Binyamin_Nitanyahu_greeting_Barack_Obama.jpg)](/wiki/File:Shimon_Peres_observes_Binyamin_Nitanyahu_greeting_Barack_Obama.jpg)

Israeli prime minister [Benjamin Netanyahu](/wiki/Benjamin_Netanyahu "Benjamin Netanyahu") meeting with President Obama in 2013

Democrats have historically been stronger supporters of Israel than Republicans.[[325]](#cite_note-Cavari-2020-328) During the 1940s, the party advocated for the cause of an independent Jewish state over the objections of many [conservatives](/wiki/Conservatism_in_the_United_States "Conservatism in the United States") in the [Old Right](/wiki/Old_Right_(United_States) "Old Right (United States)"), who strongly opposed it.[[325]](#cite_note-Cavari-2020-328) In 1948, Democratic President [Harry Truman](/wiki/Harry_S._Truman "Harry S. Truman") became the first world leader to recognize an independent state of Israel.[[326]](#cite_note-329)

The 2020 Democratic Party platform acknowledges a "commitment to Israel's security, its qualitative military edge, its right to defend itself, and the 2016 Memorandum of Understanding is ironclad" and that "we oppose any effort to unfairly single out and delegitimize Israel, including at the United Nations or through the [Boycott, Divestment, and Sanctions Movement](/wiki/Boycott,_Divestment_and_Sanctions "Boycott, Divestment and Sanctions")".[[327]](#cite_note-330) During the [Gaza war](/wiki/Gaza_war "Gaza war"), the party requested a large-scale military aid package to Israel.[[328]](#cite_note-331) Biden also announced [military support for Israel](/wiki/United_States_support_for_Israel_in_the_Gaza_war "United States support for Israel in the Gaza war"), condemned the actions of [Hamas](/wiki/Hamas "Hamas") and other Palestinian militants as terrorism,[[329]](#cite_note-332) and ordered the U.S. military to build a port to facilitate the arrival of [humanitarian aid to Palestinian civilians in Gaza](/wiki/Humanitarian_aid_during_the_Gaza_war "Humanitarian aid during the Gaza war").[[330]](#cite_note-333) However, parts of the Democratic base also became more skeptical of the Israel government.[[331]](#cite_note-334)
The number of Democrats (and Americans in general) who oppose sending arms to Israel has grown as Israel's war in Gaza has continued.[[332]](#cite_note-335) Experts said support for Israel could have hurt Democrats in several key states, including Michigan and Pennsylvania, in the 2024 presidential election.[[333]](#cite_note-336)

Late in 2024, twenty Democratic lawmakers requested support for U.S. legislation that would ban arms trade with countries that hinder humanitarian aid.[[334]](#cite_note-337)
According to a [Pew Research Center](/wiki/Pew_Research_Center "Pew Research Center") poll conducted in March 2025, 69% of Democrats have an unfavorable view of Israel, compared to 53% in 2022, before the Gaza war.[[335]](#cite_note-338) By July 2025, about half of the Democratic Senate delegation was opposed to sending arms to Israel.[[336]](#cite_note-339)

#### Europe, Russia, and Ukraine

The 2022 [Russian invasion of Ukraine](/wiki/Russian_invasion_of_Ukraine "Russian invasion of Ukraine") was politically and economically opposed by the Biden Administration, who promptly began an increased arming of Ukraine.[[337]](#cite_note-against_2022_05_19_thehill-340)[[338]](#cite_note-loud_2023_05_19_nytimes-341) In October 2023, the Biden administration requested an additional $61.4 billion in aid for Ukraine for the year ahead,[[339]](#cite_note-342) but delays in the passage of further aid by the Republican-controlled [House of Representatives](/wiki/United_States_House_of_Representatives "United States House of Representatives") inhibited progress, with the additional $61 billion in aid to Ukraine added in April 2024.[[340]](#cite_note-343)[[341]](#cite_note-344)[[342]](#cite_note-345)

## Factions

Main article: [Factions in the Democratic Party (United States)](/wiki/Factions_in_the_Democratic_Party_(United_States) "Factions in the Democratic Party (United States)")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9e/Educational_Attainment_in_the_States_won_by_Kamala_Harris_in_2024.png/330px-Educational_Attainment_in_the_States_won_by_Kamala_Harris_in_2024.png)](/wiki/File:Educational_Attainment_in_the_States_won_by_Kamala_Harris_in_2024.png)

Bar plot of the percentage of the population with a BA or higher in the electoral jurisdictions won by Kamala Harris in the 2024 United States presidential election[[343]](#cite_note-CensusData-346)

Upon foundation, the Democratic Party supported [agrarianism](/wiki/Agrarianism "Agrarianism") and the [Jacksonian democracy](/wiki/Jacksonian_democracy "Jacksonian democracy") movement of President [Andrew Jackson](/wiki/Andrew_Jackson "Andrew Jackson"), representing farmers and rural interests and traditional [Jeffersonian democrats](/wiki/Jeffersonian_democracy "Jeffersonian democracy").[[344]](#cite_note-347) Since the 1890s, especially in Northern states, the party began to favor more liberal positions (the term "liberal" in this sense describes [modern liberalism](/wiki/Modern_liberalism_in_the_United_States "Modern liberalism in the United States"), rather than [classical liberalism](/wiki/Classical_liberalism "Classical liberalism") or [economic liberalism](/wiki/Economic_liberalism "Economic liberalism")). Historically, the party has represented farmers, laborers, and religious and ethnic minorities as it has opposed unregulated business and finance and favored progressive income taxes.

In the 1930s, the party began advocating [social programs](/wiki/Social_programs_in_the_United_States "Social programs in the United States") targeted at the poor. Before the [New Deal](/wiki/New_Deal "New Deal"), the party had a [fiscally conservative](/wiki/Fiscal_conservatism "Fiscal conservatism"), [pro-business](/wiki/Economic_liberalism "Economic liberalism") wing, typified by [Grover Cleveland](/wiki/Grover_Cleveland "Grover Cleveland") and [Al Smith](/wiki/Al_Smith "Al Smith").[[345]](#cite_note-348) The party was [dominant in the Southern United States](/wiki/Solid_South "Solid South") until President [Lyndon B. Johnson](/wiki/Lyndon_B._Johnson "Lyndon B. Johnson") signed the [Civil Rights Act of 1964](/wiki/Civil_Rights_Act_of_1964 "Civil Rights Act of 1964"). In foreign policy, [internationalism](/wiki/Internationalism_(politics) "Internationalism (politics)") (including [interventionism](/wiki/Interventionism_(politics) "Interventionism (politics)")) was a dominant theme from 1913 to the mid-1960s. The major influences for liberalism were labor unions (which peaked in the 1936–1952 era) and African Americans. Environmentalism has been a major component since the 1970s.

Even after the New Deal, until the 2010s, the party still had [a fiscally conservative faction](/wiki/Conservative_coalition "Conservative coalition"),[[346]](#cite_note-blue-dog-regroup-349) such as [John Nance Garner](/wiki/John_Nance_Garner "John Nance Garner") and [Howard W. Smith](/wiki/Howard_W._Smith "Howard W. Smith").[[347]](#cite_note-350) The party's [Southern conservative](/wiki/Southern_Democrats "Southern Democrats") wing began shrinking after President [Lyndon B. Johnson](/wiki/Lyndon_B._Johnson "Lyndon B. Johnson") supported the [Civil Rights Act of 1964](/wiki/Civil_Rights_Act_of_1964 "Civil Rights Act of 1964"), and largely died out in the 2010s, as the Republican Party built up its Southern base.[[106]](#cite_note-The_long_goodbye-108)[[171]](#cite_note-Cohn-2014-174) The party still receives support from African Americans and urban areas in the Southern United States.[[348]](#cite_note-351)[[349]](#cite_note-352)

The 21st century Democratic Party is predominantly a coalition of centrists, liberals, and progressives, with significant overlap between the three groups. In 2019, the [Pew Research Center](/wiki/Pew_Research_Center "Pew Research Center") found that among Democratic and Democratic-leaning registered voters, 47% identify as liberal or very liberal, 38% identify as moderate, and 14% identify as conservative or very conservative.[[350]](#cite_note-353)[[351]](#cite_note-354) Political scientists characterize the Democratic Party as less ideologically cohesive than the Republican Party due to the broader diversity of coalitions that compose the Democratic Party.[[352]](#cite_note-Gidron-2019-355)[[353]](#cite_note-Grossman-2016-356)[[354]](#cite_note-Lelkes-2016-357)

The party has lost significant ground with voters without college degrees in the 21st century, in line with trends across the developed world. The realignment unfolded gradually, first with white voters in the [South](/wiki/Southern_United_States "Southern United States")[[171]](#cite_note-Cohn-2014-174)[[172]](#cite_note-Dogs-175) and [Midwest](/wiki/Midwestern_United_States "Midwestern United States"),[[355]](#cite_note-358) and later with voters as a whole without college degrees, except for [African Americans](/wiki/African_Americans "African Americans").[[356]](#cite_note-Where_Have_All_The_Democrats_Gone?-359)[[357]](#cite_note-360)

Democrats have consistently won voters with [graduate degrees](/wiki/Postgraduate_education "Postgraduate education") since the 1990s, including a majority of white voters with graduate degrees.[[358]](#cite_note-Polarization_by_education-361) Since the 2010s, the party's main demographic gains have been among white voters with college degrees, which until 2016 had been a Republican-leaning group.[[165]](#cite_note-Harry_Enten-168) The party still receives extremely strong support from [African Americans](/wiki/African_Americans "African Americans"), but has lost ground among other racial minorities, including [Hispanics](/wiki/Hispanic_and_Latino_Americans "Hispanic and Latino Americans"), [Native Americans](/wiki/Native_Americans_in_the_United_States "Native Americans in the United States"), and [Asian Americans](/wiki/Asian_Americans "Asian Americans").[[359]](#cite_note-Lost_Their-362)

### Liberals

See also: [Modern liberalism in the United States](/wiki/Modern_liberalism_in_the_United_States "Modern liberalism in the United States")

[![Percent of self-identified liberals by state in 2018](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/40/Self-identified_liberals_2018_Gallup.svg/330px-Self-identified_liberals_2018_Gallup.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Self-identified_liberals_2018_Gallup.svg)

[![Correlation between Educational Attainment and Liberalism by State](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a3/Correlation_between_Education_and_Liberalism.png/330px-Correlation_between_Education_and_Liberalism.png)](/wiki/File:Correlation_between_Education_and_Liberalism.png)

**Top:** Percent of self-identified liberals by state in 2018, according to a [Gallup poll](/wiki/Gallup_poll "Gallup poll").[[360]](#cite_note-Liberals-363) **Bottom:** Linear regression of educational attainment vs. liberalism by state, based on the same Gallup data.[[360]](#cite_note-Liberals-363)

Modern liberals are a large portion of the Democratic base. According to 2018 exit polls, liberals constituted 27% of the electorate, and 91% of American liberals favored the candidate of the Democratic Party.[[361]](#cite_note-2018e-364) [White-collar](/wiki/White-collar_worker "White-collar worker") college-educated professionals were mostly Republican until the 1950s, but they had become a vital component of the Democratic Party by the early 2000s.[[362]](#cite_note-Judis,_B._J._(July_11,_2003)._The_trouble_with_Howard_Dean._''Salon.com''.-365)

According to a 2025 [Gallup poll](/wiki/Gallup_poll "Gallup poll"), 37% of American voters identify as "conservative" or "very conservative", 34% as "moderate", and 25% as "liberal" or "very liberal". For Democrats, 9% identified as conservative, 34% as moderate, and 55% as liberal.[[363]](#cite_note-366)

A large majority of liberals favor moving toward [universal health care](/wiki/Universal_health_care "Universal health care"). A majority also favor diplomacy over [military action](/wiki/War "War"); [stem cell research](/wiki/Stem_cell "Stem cell"), [same-sex marriage](/wiki/Same-sex_marriage_in_the_United_States "Same-sex marriage in the United States"), stricter [gun control](/wiki/Gun_politics_in_the_United_States "Gun politics in the United States"), environmental protection laws, as well as the preservation of [abortion rights](/wiki/Pro-choice "Pro-choice"). Immigration and [cultural diversity](/wiki/Cultural_diversity "Cultural diversity") are deemed positive as liberals favor [cultural pluralism](/wiki/Cultural_pluralism "Cultural pluralism"), a system in which immigrants retain their native culture in addition to adopting their new culture. Most liberals oppose increased military spending and the mixing of church and state.[[364]](#cite_note-Pew_Research_Center.-367) As of 2020, the three most significant labor groupings in the Democratic coalition were the [AFL–CIO](/wiki/AFL%E2%80%93CIO "AFL–CIO") and [Change to Win](/wiki/Change_to_Win_Federation "Change to Win Federation") [labor federations](/wiki/National_trade_union_center "National trade union center") as well as the [National Education Association](/wiki/National_Education_Association "National Education Association"), a large, unaffiliated teachers' union. Important issues for labor unions include supporting unionized manufacturing jobs, raising the [minimum wage](/wiki/Minimum_wage_in_the_United_States "Minimum wage in the United States"), and promoting broad social programs such as [Social Security](/wiki/Social_Security_(United_States) "Social Security (United States)") and [Medicare](/wiki/Medicare_(United_States) "Medicare (United States)").[[365]](#cite_note-368)

This ideological group is strongly correlated with high educational attainment. According to the [Pew Research Center](/wiki/Pew_Research_Center "Pew Research Center"), 49% were college graduates, the highest figure of any typographical group.[[366]](#cite_note-Polarized_by_Degrees-369) It was also the fastest-growing typological group since the late 1990s to the present.[[364]](#cite_note-Pew_Research_Center.-367) Liberals include most of the academia[[367]](#cite_note-Kurtz,_H._(March_29,_2005)._College_Faculties_A_Most_Liberal_Lot,_Study_Finds._''The_Washington_Post''.-370) and large portions of the professional class.[[368]](#cite_note-nymag.com-371)

### Moderates

See also: [New Democrats (United States)](/wiki/New_Democrats_(United_States) "New Democrats (United States)"), [New Democrat Coalition](/wiki/New_Democrat_Coalition "New Democrat Coalition"), and [Blue Dog Coalition](/wiki/Blue_Dog_Coalition "Blue Dog Coalition")

Moderate Democrats, or [New Democrats](/wiki/New_Democrats_(United_States) "New Democrats (United States)"), are an ideologically [centrist](/wiki/Centrism "Centrism") faction within the Democratic Party that emerged after the victory of [Republican](/wiki/Republican_Party_(United_States) "Republican Party (United States)") [George H. W. Bush](/wiki/George_H._W._Bush "George H. W. Bush") in the [1988 presidential election](/wiki/1988_United_States_presidential_election "1988 United States presidential election").[[369]](#cite_note-372) Running as a New Democrat, Bill Clinton won the [1992](/wiki/1992_United_States_presidential_election "1992 United States presidential election") and [1996](/wiki/1996_United_States_presidential_election "1996 United States presidential election") presidential elections.[[370]](#cite_note-373) They are an economically [liberal](/wiki/Economic_liberalism "Economic liberalism") and "[Third Way](/wiki/Third_Way "Third Way")" faction that dominated the party for around 20 years, until the beginning of [Obama's presidency](/wiki/Presidency_of_Barack_Obama "Presidency of Barack Obama").[[346]](#cite_note-blue-dog-regroup-349)[[371]](#cite_note-vox.com-374) They are represented by organizations such as the [New Democrat Network](/wiki/New_Democrat_Network "New Democrat Network") and the [New Democrat Coalition](/wiki/New_Democrat_Coalition "New Democrat Coalition").

The [Blue Dog Coalition](/wiki/Blue_Dog_Coalition "Blue Dog Coalition") was formed during the [104th Congress](/wiki/104th_United_States_Congress "104th United States Congress") to give members from the Democratic Party representing conservative-leaning districts a unified voice after the Democrats' loss of Congress in the [1994](/wiki/1994_United_States_House_of_Representatives_elections "1994 United States House of Representatives elections") [Republican Revolution](/wiki/Republican_Revolution "Republican Revolution").[[372]](#cite_note-375)[[373]](#cite_note-376)[[374]](#cite_note-377) However, in the late 2010s and early 2020s, the coalition's focus shifted towards ideological [centrism](/wiki/Centrism "Centrism"). One of the most influential centrist groups was the [Democratic Leadership Council](/wiki/Democratic_Leadership_Council "Democratic Leadership Council") (DLC), a nonprofit organization that advocated centrist positions for the party. The DLC disbanded in 2011.[[375]](#cite_note-378)

Some Democratic elected officials have self-declared as being centrists, including former president Bill Clinton, former vice president [Al Gore](/wiki/Al_Gore "Al Gore"), Senator [Mark Warner](/wiki/Mark_Warner "Mark Warner"), Kansas governor [Laura Kelly](/wiki/Laura_Kelly "Laura Kelly"), former senator [Jim Webb](/wiki/Jim_Webb "Jim Webb"), and President [Joe Biden](/wiki/Joe_Biden "Joe Biden").[[376]](#cite_note-Members_—_New_Democrat_Coalition-379)[[377]](#cite_note-380) The New Democrat Network supports socially liberal and fiscally moderate Democratic politicians and is associated with the congressional [New Democrat Coalition](/wiki/New_Democrat_Coalition "New Democrat Coalition") in the House.[[378]](#cite_note-381) [Annie Kuster](/wiki/Annie_Kuster "Annie Kuster") is the chair of the coalition,[[376]](#cite_note-Members_—_New_Democrat_Coalition-379) and former senator and president [Barack Obama](/wiki/Barack_Obama "Barack Obama") was self-described as a New Democrat.[[379]](#cite_note-382) In the 21st century, some former Republican moderates have switched to the Democratic Party.[[380]](#cite_note-383)[[381]](#cite_note-384)[[382]](#cite_note-auto1-385)

### Progressives

See also: [Congressional Progressive Caucus](/wiki/Congressional_Progressive_Caucus "Congressional Progressive Caucus"), [Progressivism in the United States](/wiki/Progressivism_in_the_United_States "Progressivism in the United States"), [Social democracy](/wiki/Social_democracy "Social democracy"), and [Democratic Socialists of America](/wiki/Democratic_Socialists_of_America "Democratic Socialists of America")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/37/Alexandria_Ocasio-Cortez_%26_Bernie_Sanders_%2854401273607%29.jpg/250px-Alexandria_Ocasio-Cortez_%26_Bernie_Sanders_%2854401273607%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Alexandria_Ocasio-Cortez_%26_Bernie_Sanders_(54401273607).jpg)

[Bernie Sanders](/wiki/Bernie_Sanders "Bernie Sanders") and [Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez](/wiki/Alexandria_Ocasio-Cortez "Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez") during the [Fighting Oligarchy Tour](/wiki/Fighting_Oligarchy_Tour "Fighting Oligarchy Tour")

Progressives are the most left-leaning faction in the party and support strong business regulations, [social programs](/wiki/Social_programs_in_the_United_States "Social programs in the United States"), and [workers' rights](/wiki/Workers%27_rights "Workers' rights").[[383]](#cite_note-386)[[384]](#cite_note-387) In 2014, progressive Senator [Elizabeth Warren](/wiki/Elizabeth_Warren "Elizabeth Warren") set out "Eleven Commandments of Progressivism": tougher regulation on corporations; affordable education; scientific investment and environmentalism; [net neutrality](/wiki/Net_neutrality "Net neutrality"); increased wages; equal pay for women; collective bargaining rights; defending social programs; [same-sex marriage](/wiki/Same-sex_marriage_in_the_United_States "Same-sex marriage in the United States"); [immigration reform](/wiki/Immigration_reform_in_the_US "Immigration reform in the US"); and unabridged access to reproductive healthcare.[[385]](#cite_note-388) The [Congressional Progressive Caucus](/wiki/Congressional_Progressive_Caucus "Congressional Progressive Caucus") (CPC) is a caucus of progressive Democrats chaired by [Greg Casar](/wiki/Greg_Casar "Greg Casar") of [Texas](/wiki/Texas "Texas").[[386]](#cite_note-389)[[387]](#cite_note-Gerstle2022-390) Its members have included representatives [Dennis Kucinich](/wiki/Dennis_Kucinich "Dennis Kucinich") of [Ohio](/wiki/Ohio "Ohio"), [John Conyers](/wiki/John_Conyers "John Conyers") of [Michigan](/wiki/Michigan "Michigan"), [Jim McDermott](/wiki/Jim_McDermott "Jim McDermott") of [Washington](/wiki/Washington_(state) "Washington (state)"), [Barbara Lee](/wiki/Barbara_Lee "Barbara Lee") of California, and Senator [Paul Wellstone](/wiki/Paul_Wellstone "Paul Wellstone") of [Minnesota](/wiki/Minnesota "Minnesota"). Senators [Tammy Baldwin](/wiki/Tammy_Baldwin "Tammy Baldwin") of [Wisconsin](/wiki/Wisconsin "Wisconsin"), [Mazie Hirono](/wiki/Mazie_Hirono "Mazie Hirono") of Hawaii, and [Ed Markey](/wiki/Ed_Markey "Ed Markey") of [Massachusetts](/wiki/Massachusetts "Massachusetts") were members of the caucus when in the House of Representatives. As of 2024, the CPC is the second-largest ideological caucus in the House Democratic Caucus by voting members, behind the New Democrat Coalition.[[388]](#cite_note-391)[[389]](#cite_note-392) Senator [Bernie Sanders](/wiki/Bernie_Sanders "Bernie Sanders") has often been viewed as a leader of the progressive movement;[[390]](#cite_note-393)[[391]](#cite_note-394)[[392]](#cite_note-395) he ran presidential campaigns in [2016](/wiki/2016_Democratic_Party_presidential_primaries "2016 Democratic Party presidential primaries") and [2020](/wiki/2020_Democratic_Party_presidential_primaries "2020 Democratic Party presidential primaries").[[393]](#cite_note-396) Other members of the progressive faction include the [Squad](/wiki/Squad_(U.S._Congress) "Squad (U.S. Congress)").[[394]](#cite_note-Cornwell-397)

## Demographics

Main article: [Demographics of the Democratic Party (United States)](/wiki/Demographics_of_the_Democratic_Party_(United_States) "Demographics of the Democratic Party (United States)")

[![2020 presidential election by county](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/89/2020_Presidential_Election_by_County.svg/330px-2020_Presidential_Election_by_County.svg.png)](/wiki/File:2020_Presidential_Election_by_County.svg)

[![Majority-Black Counties in the U.S. as of the 2020 United States Census](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/62/2020_Census_-_Majority-Black_Counties_in_the_United_States.png/330px-2020_Census_-_Majority-Black_Counties_in_the_United_States.png)](/wiki/File:2020_Census_-_Majority-Black_Counties_in_the_United_States.png)

**Top to bottom:**

[2020 presidential election](/wiki/2020_United_States_presidential_election "2020 United States presidential election") by county; Majority-Black Counties in the U.S. as of the [2020 United States Census](/wiki/2020_United_States_Census "2020 United States Census")

In the [2024 presidential election](/wiki/2024_United_States_presidential_election "2024 United States presidential election"), the party performed best among voters who were [upper income](/wiki/Affluence_in_the_United_States "Affluence in the United States"),[[395]](#cite_note-:0-398)[[359]](#cite_note-Lost_Their-362)[[396]](#cite_note-Too_Rich-399) lived in [urban areas](/wiki/Urban%E2%80%93rural_political_divide "Urban–rural political divide"),[[397]](#cite_note-McGreal-400)[[398]](#cite_note-cities-401) [college graduates](/wiki/Educational_attainment_in_the_United_States "Educational attainment in the United States"),[[358]](#cite_note-Polarization_by_education-361)[[366]](#cite_note-Polarized_by_Degrees-369)[[368]](#cite_note-nymag.com-371)[[399]](#cite_note-nytimes.com-402)[[400]](#cite_note-Fallen_Behind-403) identified as [Atheist](/wiki/Atheism "Atheism"), [Agnostic](/wiki/Agnosticism "Agnosticism"), or [Jewish](/wiki/American_Jews "American Jews"); [African Americans](/wiki/African_Americans "African Americans"),[[401]](#cite_note-Blacks_and_the_Democratic_Party-404)[[402]](#cite_note-Bositis-405) [LGBTQ+](/wiki/LGBTQ%2B "LGBTQ+"), and [unmarried](/wiki/Marital_status "Marital status").[[91]](#cite_note-Activists_and_Partisan_Realignment-93)[[182]](#cite_note-Grossmann-2021-185)[[403]](#cite_note-pewresearch.org-406) In particular, [Kamala Harris](/wiki/Kamala_Harris "Kamala Harris")' two strongest demographic groups in the 2024 presidential election were African Americans (86–13%) and LGBT voters (86–12%).[[404]](#cite_note-2024_Exit_poll-407)

Support for the [civil rights movement](/wiki/Civil_rights_movement "Civil rights movement") in the 1960s by Democratic presidents [John F. Kennedy](/wiki/John_F._Kennedy "John F. Kennedy") and [Lyndon B. Johnson](/wiki/Lyndon_B._Johnson "Lyndon B. Johnson") helped increase the Democrats' support within the African-American community. African Americans have consistently voted between 85% and 95% Democratic since the 1960s, making African Americans one of the largest of the party's constituencies.[[401]](#cite_note-Blacks_and_the_Democratic_Party-404)[[402]](#cite_note-Bositis-405)

According to the [Pew Research Center](/wiki/Pew_Research_Center "Pew Research Center"), 78.4% of Democrats in the 116th United States Congress were Christian.[[405]](#cite_note-408) However, the vast majority of white evangelical and [Latter-day Saint](/wiki/The_Church_of_Jesus_Christ_of_Latter-day_Saints "The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints") Christians favor the [Republican Party](/wiki/Republican_Party_(United_States) "Republican Party (United States)").[[406]](#cite_note-409) The party also receives strong support from [non-religious](/wiki/Irreligion "Irreligion") voters.[[407]](#cite_note-410)[[408]](#cite_note-411)

### Age

[Younger Americans](/wiki/Youth_vote_in_the_United_States "Youth vote in the United States") have tended to vote mainly for Democratic candidates in recent years, particularly those under the age of 30.[[409]](#cite_note-trends-412)

In the 2024 presidential election, Harris won voters aged 18–29 (54–43%) and 30–39 (51–45%), tied among those aged 40–49 (49–49%), lost those aged 50–64 (43–56%), and narrowly lost those aged 65 and older (49–50%). The median voter is in their 50s.[[410]](#cite_note-413)

One of the main reasons that 18– to 29-year-old voters strongly support Democrats is that they are much less likely to be married. Harris tied Donald Trump with white voters aged 18 to 29 (49-49%) and won white women aged 18 to 29 (54-44%).[[411]](#cite_note-Single_Women-414)

### Race

[![White vote in the 2020 presidential election by state](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d3/White_voters.jpg/330px-White_voters.jpg)](/wiki/File:White_voters.jpg)

**Top to bottom:**

White vote in the [2020 presidential election](/wiki/2020_United_States_presidential_election "2020 United States presidential election") by state and county.[[412]](#cite_note-White_Voters-415)[[171]](#cite_note-Cohn-2014-174)

Referring to the state map of the white vote, Kamala Harris in [2024](/wiki/2024_United_States_presidential_election "2024 United States presidential election") won every state in which Joe Biden had won the white vote in [2020](/wiki/2020_United_States_presidential_election "2020 United States presidential election"). Republican Donald Trump won every state in which Joe Biden had lost the white vote except for [Virginia](/wiki/Virginia "Virginia").[[412]](#cite_note-White_Voters-415) Virginia is 20% African American, and its white voters are much less Republican than those of other Southern states because [Northern Virginia](/wiki/Northern_Virginia "Northern Virginia"), which is in the [Washington metropolitan area](/wiki/Washington_metropolitan_area "Washington metropolitan area"), is a Democratic stronghold.[[413]](#cite_note-416)

Referring to the county map of the white vote, Democrats carry white voters in most of [New England](/wiki/New_England "New England") and the [West Coast](/wiki/West_Coast_of_the_United_States "West Coast of the United States"). Democrats also do well in regions with high [Nordic and Scandinavian ancestry](/wiki/Nordic_and_Scandinavian_Americans "Nordic and Scandinavian Americans"). For example, this keeps white voters in Minnesota and Wisconsin much less Republican than in other Midwestern states.[[414]](#cite_note-417)[[415]](#cite_note-418)

Democrats are also relatively competitive among or win white voters in parts of the [Northeast](/wiki/Northeastern_United_States "Northeastern United States"), [Midwest](/wiki/Midwestern_United_States "Midwestern United States"), and [Southwest](/wiki/Southwestern_United_States "Southwestern United States"). Democrats do particularly poorly among [white Southerners](/wiki/White_Southerners "White Southerners"), as racial polarization is extremely high in the Southern United States.[[412]](#cite_note-White_Voters-415)

In the 2024 presidential election, African Americans supported Kamala Harris 86-13%, while white Southerners supported Donald Trump 67-32%. Even in many urban counties in the Southern United States, Democrats do not win a majority of white voters. Trump won both white Southerners with college degrees (57-41%) and without college degrees (75-24%).[[412]](#cite_note-White_Voters-415)

* In the swing states of Georgia and North Carolina, which Harris lost by 2.2% and 3.2%, respectively, whites supported Trump 71-28% and 62-37%. Trump won white voters with college degrees in Georgia 57-43%, and lost white voters with college degrees in North Carolina 47–51%.
* White evangelicals supported Trump in Georgia (91-9%) and North Carolina (87-12%), on par with African-American support for Harris in Georgia (88-11%) and North Carolina (86-12%).[[404]](#cite_note-2024_Exit_poll-407)

[New Mexico](/wiki/New_Mexico "New Mexico") is almost [half Hispanic](/wiki/Hispanics_and_Latinos_in_New_Mexico "Hispanics and Latinos in New Mexico") (49.3%), and is the most heavily Hispanic state in the country.[[416]](#cite_note-419) Of the 19 states and the District of Columbia won by Kamala Harris in the 2024 presidential election, all except New Mexico had above-average educational attainment.[[343]](#cite_note-CensusData-346) New Mexico also had the lowest [population density](/wiki/List_of_states_and_territories_of_the_United_States_by_population_density "List of states and territories of the United States by population density") and the highest [poverty rate](/wiki/List_of_U.S._states_and_territories_by_poverty_rate "List of U.S. states and territories by poverty rate") of any state carried by Harris.

### Gender and sexual minorities

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c2/Total_Fertility_Rate_by_U.S._state.svg/330px-Total_Fertility_Rate_by_U.S._state.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Total_Fertility_Rate_by_U.S._state.svg)

Total Fertility Rate by U.S. state in 2021

Since 1980, a "gender gap" has seen stronger support for the Democratic Party among women than among men. Unmarried and divorced women are more likely to vote for Democrats.[[417]](#cite_note-wvwv2004-420)[[418]](#cite_note-421) Although women supported Obama over [Mitt Romney](/wiki/Mitt_Romney "Mitt Romney") by a margin of 55–44% in 2012, Romney prevailed amongst married women, 53–46%.[[419]](#cite_note-Marriage_Gap-422) Obama won unmarried women 67–31%.[[420]](#cite_note-423) According to a December 2019 study, "White women are the only group of female voters who support Republican Party candidates for president. They have done so by a majority in all but 2 of the last 18 elections".[[421]](#cite_note-424)[[422]](#cite_note-425)

In the 2024 presidential election, LGBTQ+ voters supported Harris 86-12%, on par with African Americans. Harris lost married men (38–60%) and married women (47–52%), tied among unmarried men (48-48%), and won unmarried women (61-38%).[[411]](#cite_note-Single_Women-414)

White women with college degrees do support Democrats somewhat strongly, with Harris winning them 58-41%, likely the best modern performance with this demographic. They were one of the few demographic groups that shifted towards Democrats from 2020 to 2024.[[423]](#cite_note-gap_within_the_gender_gap-426)

[Total fertility rate](/wiki/List_of_U.S._states_and_territories_by_fertility_rate "List of U.S. states and territories by fertility rate") is strongly negatively correlated with support for the Democratic Party. Specifically, as total fertility increased in states, Democratic vote share decreased.[[411]](#cite_note-Single_Women-414)

### Region

See also: [Solid South](/wiki/Solid_South "Solid South")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/08/Hispanic_and_Latino_Americans_by_state.svg/330px-Hispanic_and_Latino_Americans_by_state.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Hispanic_and_Latino_Americans_by_state.svg)

Proportion of Americans who are Hispanic or Latino in each U.S. state, [DC](/wiki/District_of_Columbia "District of Columbia"), and Puerto Rico as of the 2020 United States Census

Geographically, the party is strongest in the [Northeastern United States](/wiki/Northeastern_United_States "Northeastern United States"), parts of the [Great Lakes region](/wiki/Great_Lakes_region "Great Lakes region") and [Southwestern United States](/wiki/Southwestern_United_States "Southwestern United States"), and the [West Coast](/wiki/West_Coast_of_the_United_States "West Coast of the United States"). The party is also very strong in [major cities](/wiki/List_of_United_States_cities_by_population "List of United States cities by population"), regardless of region.[[398]](#cite_note-cities-401)

The Democratic Party gradually lost its power in the [Southern United States](/wiki/Southern_United_States "Southern United States") since [1964](/wiki/1964_United_States_presidential_election "1964 United States presidential election"). Although [Richard Nixon](/wiki/Richard_Nixon "Richard Nixon") carried 49 states in [1972](/wiki/1972_United_States_presidential_election "1972 United States presidential election"), including every Southern state, the Republican Party remained quite weak at the local and state levels across the entire South for decades. Republicans first won a majority of U.S. House seats in the South in the [1994](/wiki/1994_United_States_elections "1994 United States elections") "[Republican Revolution](/wiki/Republican_Revolution "Republican Revolution")", and only began to dominate the South after the [2010 elections](/wiki/2010_United_States_elections "2010 United States elections").[[106]](#cite_note-The_long_goodbye-108) Since the 2010s, [white Southerners](/wiki/White_Southerners "White Southerners") have been the Republican Party's strongest racial demographic, in some [Deep South](/wiki/Deep_South "Deep South") states voting nearly as Republican as African Americans vote Democratic.[[171]](#cite_note-Cohn-2014-174) This is partially attributable to religiosity, with white [evangelical Christians](/wiki/Evangelical_Christianity "Evangelical Christianity") in the [Bible Belt](/wiki/Bible_Belt "Bible Belt"), which covers most of the South, being the Republican Party's strongest religious demographic.

The Democratic Party is particularly strong in the West Coast and the Northeastern United States. In particular, the Democratic Party receives its strongest support from white voters in these two regions. This is attributable to the two regions having the highest educational attainment in the country and being part of the "[Unchurched Belt](/wiki/Unchurched_Belt "Unchurched Belt")", with the lowest rates of religiosity in the country.[[412]](#cite_note-White_Voters-415)

The Democratic Party's support in the [Midwest](/wiki/Midwestern_United_States "Midwestern United States") and [Southwest](/wiki/Southwestern_United_States "Southwestern United States") are more mixed, with varying levels of support from white voters in both regions. In the Midwest, the Democratic Party receives varying levels of support, with some states safely Democratic, some [swing states](/wiki/Swing_states "Swing states"), and some safely Republican. In the Southwest, the Democratic Party also relies on [Hispanic](/wiki/Hispanic_and_Latino_Americans "Hispanic and Latino Americans") voters.[[424]](#cite_note-The_New_West-427)

The Democratic Party is particularly weak in the [Great Plains](/wiki/Great_Plains "Great Plains") and some [Mountain states](/wiki/Mountain_states "Mountain states"). In particular, the states of [Idaho](/wiki/Idaho "Idaho"), [Utah](/wiki/Utah "Utah"), [Wyoming](/wiki/Wyoming "Wyoming"), [North Dakota](/wiki/North_Dakota "North Dakota"), [South Dakota](/wiki/South_Dakota "South Dakota"), [Nebraska](/wiki/Nebraska "Nebraska"),[[d]](#cite_note-428) [Kansas](/wiki/Kansas "Kansas"), and [Oklahoma](/wiki/Oklahoma "Oklahoma") have not voted for the Democratic Party since the [1964 presidential election](/wiki/1964_United_States_presidential_election "1964 United States presidential election"). [Montana](/wiki/Montana "Montana") has not voted for the Democratic Party since the [1992 presidential election](/wiki/1992_United_States_presidential_election "1992 United States presidential election").[[425]](#cite_note-how-429)
